title: INTRODUCTION TO BIOLOGICAL INQUIRY
description: Development of the skills and attitudes necessary for understanding and conducting biological inquiry. A problem-solving approach will be used to train students to make critical observations, to formulate hypotheses, to design experiments, and to choose appropriate analyses. Three hours of lecture.

title: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
description: This is a course that will cover the structural and functional changes in cells, tissues, and organs of the body that are caused by disease and the effects that these changes have on total body function. Attention will also be placed on the mechanisms of the underlying disease processes and the clinical manifestations of the diseases, as well as diagnosis and treatment of many of the diseases that will be covered.

title: GENERAL ZOOLOGY
description: A study of the central facts and principles needed to understand the form, function, and evolution of animals. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 120 , BIOS 120L , and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 222L

title: GENERAL ZOOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Laboratory study of the form and function of animals. This course is designed to correlate with and supplement BIOS 222 . Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 222

title: GENERAL BOTANY
description: A study of the concepts, facts and principles central to understanding the form, function, classification, and evolution of photosynthetic Protista, higher plants and fungi. Three hours of lecture.

title: GENERAL BOTANY LABORATORY
description: Laboratory study of the form and function of plants and fungi. This course is designed to correlate with and supplement BIOS 224 . Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 224

title: FIELD ZOOLOGY
description: A basic course in field collecting techniques of all major animal phyla. Topics will include collecting techniques, specimen preparation, storage and cataloging. Five hours of lecture-lab.

title: HISTOLOGICAL TECHNIQUE
description: Study of the methods and principles of preparation of animal and plant material for microscopic examination, histochemical techniques, staining, microscopy, photomicrography, and isolation of cells and organelles. Two hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 , BIOS 224 , and credit or registration in BIOS 231L

title: HISTOLOGICAL TECHNIQUE LABORATORY
description: The practice of histochemical techniques including staining, microscopy, photomicrography, and isolation of cells and organelles. Six hours of lab.

title: DENDROLOGY
description: An identification classification, characteristics, and distribution of the principal forest trees of the United States with particular emphasis on coniferous species. One hour of lecture and three hours of lab.

title: LIVESTOCK JUDGING
description: The theory and practice of judging livestock. May be repeated for a maximum of three credits.

title: FARM CROPS
description: Fundamental principles of crop production.

title: FARM CROPS LAB
description: Supplementary to AGRI 121 by means of student field and laboratory observations and experimentations with seed and plants.

title: ELEMENTS OF DAIRYING
description: Fundamentals of dairy production and manufacturing.

title: GENERAL HORTICULTURE
description: The propagation and management of flowers, fruits, and vegetables.

title: HORTICULTURAL PRACTICES
description: An in-depth study of the modern techniques and technology practiced in the production of flowers, fruits, ornamentals and vegetables.
others: {'class_periods': 'Three-hour class periods, a combination of lecture and lab.'}

title: SOILS
description: Fundamental principles of soil science and the properties of soils as related to plant growth.
prerequisites: CHEM 112 and credit or registration in AGRI 251L

title: SOILS LABORATORY
description: Supplementary to AGRI 251 by means of analysis of soils and study of land descriptions.

title: BREED OF FARM ANIMALS
description: The origin and characteristics of the leading breeds of beef cattle, sheep, swine, and horses; development, adaptability and distribution; breed types, organizations and publications; comparative judging of representative animals.

title: BREED OF FARM ANIMALS LABORATORY
description: Supplementary to AGRI 271 by means of field and laboratory study of farm animals.

title: GOVERNMENTAL AND NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATION ACCOUNTING
description: Budgeting, fiscal processes, and financial record keeping and reporting of governmental bodies and private non-profit institutions. This course includes a research project that involves accounting aspects of nonprofit institutions.
prerequisites: ACCT 309 with a grade of C or better

title: FUNDAMENTALS OF ACCOUNTING
description: An introduction to the foundations of accounting.
others: {'grading': 'Grading will be on a pass/fail basis'}

title: FOUNDATIONS OF ADULT EDUCATION
description: An introduction to the major areas of theory, research, and practice in the field of adult education. Includes history and contemporary practices in history, philosophy, politics and policy in adult education.

title: ADULT DEVELOPMENT AND LEARNING
description: The course focus is on adult development and learning principles. Adult learning styles and motivation to learn are discussed in the context of designing effective instructional strategies appropriate in various workforce education venues.

title: INTRODUCTION TO TESTS AND MEASUREMENTS: APPLICATIONS
description: Study of the typical methods of standardized assessment. Emphasis is placed on the interpretation and communication of the results of standardized assessment instruments. Elementary statistical terms and methods are studied. As an introductory course in tests and measurement, it does not prepare students to administer or interpret psychological tests.

title: GUIDANCE/COUNSELING IN ADULT EDUCATION
description: An overview of counseling in adult education. Topics include subject matter of counseling, self-care, personal/technical tools needed for career success, and current career issues and controversies. Application of career development theory.

title: CAREER COUNSELING AND DEVELOPMENT
description: Career development theories and the career decision making process. Special emphasis is placed on strategies used by education counselors to assist their clients in making career and educational decisions. Students learn how to encourage motivation by connecting personal values and interests with academics. Topics may include multicultural considerations, the relationship between one’s career development and other life roles, assessment instruments relevant to career planning, and the process of career development.

title: TEACHING ADULT LEARNERS
description: Practical course in how adult literacy develops and what strategies work most effectively when teaching adults. Topics include how adults learn from text, learning styles and their effects on comprehension, readability, and strategies to enhance learning.

title: SOCIAL AND CULTURAL ISSUES IN EDUCATION COUNSELING
description: This course is designed to familiarize education counseling professionals with the environmental, personal, socioeconomic, and psychological characteristics of culturally different groups and to help them understand the unique counseling concerns related to varying racial and cultural groups.

title: PRACTICUM IN EDUCATION COUNSELING
description: One hundred (100) clock hours of supervised instruction and/or administration in an adult educational setting and includes orientation to the roles, responsibilities, and functions of professionals in educational counseling.

title: TAX PROBLEMS AND RESEARCH
description: This course includes research on tax questions of individuals, corporations, and partnerships.
prerequisites: ACCT 320 or consent of the Department

title: AUDITING THEORY
description: Study of the independent auditor’s contribution to reliable financial reporting including the following topics: professional ethics, legal liability, auditing standards and objectives, evidence collection, internal controls, audit sampling, and audit reports.
prerequisites: ACCT 309 with a grade of C or better

title: FRAUD EXAMINATION
description: An in-depth examination of the principles and practices for investigating and uncovering allegations of fraud and financial misconduct.

title: GOVERNMENTAL AND NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATION ACCOUNTING
description: Budgeting, fiscal processes, and financial record keeping and reporting of governmental bodies and private non-profit institutions.
prerequisites: ACCT 309 with a grade of C or better

title: ACCOUNTING THEORY
description: An examination of current accounting topics in income measurement and statement presentation.

title: INTERNSHIP IN ACCOUNTING
description: The director works with the student to find a professional internship experience that matches the student’s major. The student will not receive credit for an existing job. The student is required to write a report that details the objectives, progress, and completion of the internship. Three hours of credit on a pass/no credit basis. May be repeated for a maximum of six hours. Only three hours may be applied to the major. The student must complete at least 120 hours of work.
prerequisites: Successful completion of 60 credit hours toward the student’s degree, and either a 2.75 cumulative GPA or a 3.00 GPA for the last 24 credit hours, and approval of the business internship director

title: SEMINAR IN ACCOUNTING
description: May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours. Selected topics will vary from term to term.
prerequisites: Junior standing and consent of the Department

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES IN ACCOUNTING
description: May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours covering different topics. Readings, conferences, and reports under the guidance of a member of the accounting faculty.
prerequisites: ACCT 309 with a C or better and consent of the Department

title: TAX PROBLEMS AND RESEARCH
description: Research on tax questions related to individuals, corporations, and partnerships. Students are required to write a research paper on a tax topic to be assigned by the Instructor.
prerequisites: ACCT 320 or consent of the Department

title: FRAUD EXAMINATION
description: An in-depth examination of the principles and practices for investigating and uncovering allegations of fraud and financial misconduct with emphasis on contemporary research in fraud.

title: INTRODUCTION TO RADIO ASTRONOMY
description: An introduction to the radio universe and the tools and techniques used to explore it. Four hours of lecture/lab.

title: VISTAS IN BIOCHEMISTRY
description: An elementary introduction to Biochemistry. Topics include biomolecular structure, intermediary metabolism, and molecular genetics. One hour of lecture.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY I
description: The chemical structure and biological properties of proteins, lipids and carbohydrates and their contribution to cellular chemistry in terms of enzyme kinetics, bioenergetics and common metabolic chemistries. Three hours of lecture.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY I LABORATORY
description: An introduction to lipid, protein and carbohydrate biochemistry through the experimental investigation of their structures and functional properties. One hour lecture and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit for or registration in BCHM 410
others: {'louisiana_common_course': 'CBIO 3401/Biochemistry I Lab (Upper Level)', 'lecture_lab_hours': 'One hour lecture and two hours of lab'}

title: BIOCHEMISTRY II
description: Structure, function, and biosynthesis of cellular macromolecules and macromolecular structures. A biochemical description of the genetic apparatus of procaryotic and eukaryotic cells will be emphasized. Three hours of lecture.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY II LABORATORY
description: Isolation and characterization of proteins and nucleic acids using centrifugation, ion exchange and gel permeation chromatography, gel electrophoresis, spectrophotometry, enzymology, radiochemistry and gene cloning. One hour of lecture and two hours of lab.
others: {'louisiana_common_course': 'CBIO 4411/Biochemistry II Lab (Upper Level)', 'lecture_lab_hours': 'One hour of lecture and two hours of lab'}

title: BIOPHYSICAL CHEMISTRY
description: An introduction to chemical thermodynamics and kinetics, binding phenomena and spectroscopy with emphasis on applications in biology. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: CHEM 266 , PHYS 252 or PHYS 262 , BIOS 120 , and MATH 221

title: CLINICAL BIOCHEMISTRY
description: Clinical Biochemistry is an advanced study of biochemistry applied to the diagnosis of human disease. Measurement of the concentration of specific biomolecules in human blood, urine, and cerebrospinal fluid provides important information to the practicing physician for the diagnosis and monitoring of a wide variety of pathological conditions. This course is designed to present the biochemical causes, effects, and clinical utility of measurements of those biomolecules. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BCHM 410 with a grade of C or better and credit or concurrent enrollment in BCHM 412 , or consent of the Department

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN BIOCHEMISTRY
description: In-depth study of special areas in biochemistry. May be repeated when topic varies. One to three hours of lecture.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY IN BIOCHEMISTRY
description: Laboratory and/or library research in biochemistry. Three hours of lab per credit hour. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of 6 credit hours
others: {'lab_hours': 'Three hours of lab per credit hour', 'repeatability': 'May be repeated for credit for a maximum of 6 credit hours'}

title: PLANTS AND SOCIETY
description: A study of the significance of plants and similar organisms to human life with emphasis on how plants have affected the development of culture and society through agriculture, drugs, and medicine. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 101 , BIOS 120 , or consent of the Department

title: HUMAN HEREDITY AND SOCIETY
description: Introduction to the impact of genetics and biological research on man and his society. Includes the social, ethical, and legal implications of genetic disorders and research. Designed for the non-science student. Credit will not be allowed for use in any curricula offered by 228 the Department of Biological Sciences. Three hours of lecture and/or seminar.
prerequisites: BIOS 101 , BIOS 120 , or consent of the Department

title: HUMANS&THEIR ENVIRONMENT
description: A study of the effects of humankind’s activity upon the physical and biological environment. Designed for the non-science student. Credit will not be allowed for use in any curricula offered by the Department of Biological Sciences. Three hours of lecture and/or seminar. Students receiving credit for BIOS 107 cannot receive credit for ENSC 101 .
prerequisites: BIOS 101 , BIOS 120 , or consent of the Department

title: SOCIOBIOLOGY
description: A biological approach to social awareness. Emphasis placed on the implications of a biological basis to social issues such as parenting, sexism, racism, eugenics, human diversity, man’s antecedents, origin of life, and the bioethics of such practices as recombinant DNA technology, and genetic engineering. Designed for the non-science student. Credit will not be allowed for use in any curricula offered by the Department of Biological Sciences. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 101 , BIOS 120 , or consent of the Department

title: ANIMAL DIVERSITY
description: A study of the significance of animals to human life with emphasis on human health, agriculture, culture and societies. Credit will not be allowed for use in any curricula offered by the Department of Biological Sciences. Designed for the non-science student. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 101 , BIOS 120 , or consent of the Department

title: BIOLOGY PRINCIPLES I
description: First course in a two-part Introduction to basic biological principles for the science major. Topics include ecosystems, evolution, systematics, and basic physiology. Three hours of lecture.

title: BIOLOGICAL PRINCIPLES I LABORATORY
description: Laboratory supplement to BIOS 110 involving utilization of the scientific method to investigate the principles of ecosystem dynamics, evolution, plant and animal systematics, and basic physiology. Three hours of lab.

title: BIOLOGY PRINCIPLES II
description: Second course in a two-part introduction to the basic biological principles for the science major. Topics include molecular structure-function, cellular metabolism, genetics, and gene expression. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 110 and MATH 121 , or consent of the Department

title: BIOLOGICAL PRINCIPLES II LABORATORY
description: Laboratory supplement to BIOS 120 involving utilization of the scientific method to investigate the principles of biochemistry, metabolism, genetics, and cell biology. Three hours of lecture.

title: HEALTH SCIENCE SEMINAR
description: Seminar for undergraduate students anticipating medical studies. One hour of seminar.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF ADULT EDUCATION

Includes history and contemporary practices in history, philosophy, politics and policy in adult education. This course assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis of a more complex nature than is true for the undergraduate equivalent.

This course assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis of a more complex nature than is true for the undergraduate equivalent.

title: INTRODUCTION TO AGRICULTURE
description: A survey course covering all fields of agriculture with emphasis on opportunities and educational requirements.

title: FUNDAMENTALS OF ANIMAL HUSBANDRY
description: An introductory course dealing with beef cattle, sheep, swine, and horses and their role in American agriculture.

title: CONSERVATION OF FOREST RESOURCES
description: An introductory study of the resources of forest and rangelands including wood, wildlife, recreation, forage, and water. Elementary techniques of multiple use management of forestlands.

title: INTRODUCTORY FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING
description: An introductory course intended to familiarize the student with principles and methods of accounting primarily concerned with financial data gathering and presentation in the form of general-purpose external financial statements.
others: {'three_hours_of_lecture': 'Three hours of lecture.', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CACC 2113/Introduction to Financial Accounting'}

title: INTRODUCTORY MANAGERIAL ACCOUNTING
description: An introductory course intended to familiarize the student with principles and methods of accounting primarily concerned with data gathering and presentation for purposes of internal management evaluation and decision-making.
others: {'three_hours_of_lecture': 'Three hours of lecture.', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CACC 2213/Introduction to Managerial Accounting'}

title: ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS
description: An introduction to the concepts and practices of financial accounting and reporting. The student is introduced to (1) the role of financial reporting in the allocation of capital in a market economy, and (2) the governing principles, practices, applications, and authoritative literature used in the preparation of and interpretation of financial reports, and (3) techniques and tools used in the analysis of financial reports. Satisfies the MBA foundation accounting requirement. Credit will not be allowed for undergraduate business majors.

title: INTERMEDIATE ACCOUNTING I
description: Concepts, theories, processes and standards underlying financial accounting and reporting, including Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. Topics include the conceptual framework of accounting, the standard-setting process, the time value of money, current assets (cash, receivables, and inventories), noncurrent assets (fixed assets, depreciation, intangible assets, and investments), and cash flow.
prerequisites: ACCT 205 and ACCT 206 with a grade of C or better in each course

title: INTERMEDIATE ACCOUNTING II
description: Building upon Intermediate Accounting I with data analysis, financial statement presentation, disclosures, alternative financial statements, investments, ethical considerations, financial incentives, measurements, changes, corrections, and accounting for income taxes. Topics include liabilities, revenues, investments, stockholder equity, and earnings per share.
prerequisites: ACCT 309 with a grade of C or better

title: INTRODUCTION TO FEDERAL INCOME TAX
description: The study and research of the Federal tax structure and its effects on individuals.
prerequisites: ACCT 206 with a grade of C or better or consent of the Department

title: COST ACCOUNTING
description: A study of the collection and utilization of cost data for financial purposes and managerial decision-making processes. Computer applications, quantitative methods, and various philosophies of accounting theory are considered.
prerequisites: ACCT 206 with a grade of C or better or consent of the Department

title: OIL & GAS ACCOUNTING
description: An introduction to the current accounting practices associated with the oil and gas industry.

title: ACCOUNTING INFORMATION SYSTEMS
description: A course focusing on the use of technology for accounting. Analysis and design of standard accounting systems; emphasis on computerized systems and internal control issues.
prerequisites: ACCT 309 with a grade of C or better

title: ADVANCED FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING I
description: A study of business combinations through asset and stock acquisitions as well as consolidations of corporate groups.
prerequisites: ACCT 310 with a grade of C or better

title: INTRODUCTORY NEUROBIOLOGY
description: This is an introductory neurobiology course that provides a general background of the organization of the brain, the functioning of neurons and other cell types in the brain, and how these cells interact to permit proper brain function. Focus will be placed on cell and molecular biological aspects. Some emphasis will be placed on brain disorders resulting from the improper function of neurons and other cell types.

title: VASCULAR PLANT MORPHOLOGY
description: A survey of vascular plants, combining the basic concepts of vascular plant morphology with organography, anatomy, life histories, and evolutionary relationships.
prerequisites: BIOS 224 and credit or registration in BIOS 340L

title: VASCULAR PLANT MORPHOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Provides the opportunity to examine living, preserved and fossil specimens of all major vascular plant groups. It is designed to both correlate and supplement BIOS 340.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 340

title: INTRODUCTION TO MARINE ZOOLOGY
description: Survey of marine animals, particularly those of the LA Gulf Coast, including classification, morphology, and ecology.
others: {'semesters_offered': 'Offered summers only at the LA Universities Marine Consortium (LUMCON) facility'}

title: PLANT PHYSIOLOGY
description: The study of plant function, including soil and water relationships, plant biochemistry, development and environmental physiology.
prerequisites: BIOS 224 , CHEM 265 , and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 345L

title: PLANT PHYSIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Introduction to laboratory techniques of investigation of plant physiological function.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 345

title: PLANT PATHOLOGY
description: A study of diseases in plants, the role of environment in disease development, the genetic and biochemical nature of host-parasite interactions, and the methods of disease control. Emphasis will be placed on diseases affecting crops and ornamentals in Louisiana.

title: PLANT PATH LABORATORY
description: A course designed to provide the laboratory training necessary to study disease in plants, the role of the environment in disease development, the genetic and biochemical nature of host-parasite interactions, and the methods of disease control.

title: MEDICAL MICROBIOLOGY
description: An introduction to the essentials of Medical Microbiology with emphasis on antimicrobial agents, immunology, bacteria, fungi, viruses, and medical parasitology.

title: MEDICAL MICROBIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: A comprehensive course in identification of medically important bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoans, helminths and arthropod vectors.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 351
others: {'lab_hours': 'One hour of lab instruction and two hours of lab'}

title: DENDROLOGY II
description: A continuation of BIOS 277 with particular emphasis on hardwood species with spring and summer characteristics of forest vegetation.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'three hours of lab'}

title: HUMAN ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY I
description: The elements of human anatomy and physiology, a study of the structure and function of the major organ systems in humans, with emphasis on cells and tissues, and on the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, and nervous systems.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CBIO 2213/Human Anatomy and Physiology I'}

title: HUMAN ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY I LABORATORY
description: Experiments designed to impart a basic understanding of human anatomy and physiology using demonstrations of physiological response, human skeletons, preserved organs, slides, and dissection as needed to supplement Human Anatomy and Physiology I.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 284
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CBIO 2211/Human Anatomy and Physiology I Lab'}

title: HUMAN ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY II
description: The elements of human anatomy and physiology, a study of the structure and function of the major organ systems in humans, with emphasis on the endocrine, circulatory, respiratory, lymphatic, digestive, excretory, and reproductive systems.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CBIO 2223/Human Anatomy and Physiology II'}

title: HUMAN ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY II LABORATORY
description: Experiments designed to impart a basic understanding of human anatomy and physiology using demonstrations of physiological responses, human skeletons, preserved organs, slides, and dissection as needed to supplement Human Anatomy and Physiology II.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 285
others: {'lab_hours': 'One hour of lab', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CBIO 2221/Human Anatomy and Physiology II Lab'}

title: FIELD COURSE IN BIOLOGY
description: Study of the flora and fauna and community characteristics of different biotic environments, including developing skills in the collection and preservation of organisms for scientific study. 230 Fieldwork will be done during a two-week (approximate) field trip.
others: {'lab_hours': 'One hour of lab instruction and six hours of lab', 'repeatability': 'May be repeated for credit'}

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY
description: Introduction to laboratory and library research. Grading in this course is on a pass/no credit basis. May be repeated.
prerequisites: BIOS 120 or BIOS 120L , and consent of the Department
others: {'conference_hours': 'One hour of conference', 'independent_research_hours': 'two hours of independent research', 'grading': 'pass/no credit basis', 'repeatability': 'May be repeated'}

title: SYSTEMATICS INTERNSHIP
description: Practical experience in the curation and management of biological collections in the Museum of Life Sciences. May be repeated.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 or BIOS 224 , and consent of the Department
others: {'conference_hours': 'One hour of conference', 'curatorial_work_hours': 'two hours of curatorial work in the museum', 'repeatability': 'May be repeated'}

title: GENERAL MICROBIOLOGY
description: A basic study of viruses, rickettsiae, chlamydians, bacteria, algae, fungi, and protozoa, with emphasis on form, structure, reproduction, physiology, metabolism, and identification of microorganisms.

title: GENERAL MICROBIO LABORATORY
description: The experiments in the laboratory are integrated with lecture for an introductory course in microbiology. Emphasis will be on microscopy, microbial morphology, staining methods, use of various microbial media, pure culture techniques, biochemical activities of various microbes, characterization of unknown bacteria cultures, and bacterial and animal virus propagation.
others: {'lab_hours': 'One hour of lab instruction, and two hours of lab'}

title: DINOSAURS
description: A broad study of the major groups of dinosaurs and related reptiles of the world, including the biology, morphology, taxonomy, and ecology of each group. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Twelve semester hours of biology or consent of the Department

title: GENERAL ENTOMOLOGY
description: A systematic study of the biology of insects and related arthropods including their anatomy, morphology, physiology, development, taxonomy, ecology, and life histories. Additional emphasis will be placed on the relation of insects to humans in agriculture and medicine. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 360L

title: GENERAL ENTOMOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Field and laboratory study of the characteristics, ecology, life histories, collection, and preservation of insects. Six hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 360

title: PRINCIPLES OF GENETICS
description: Fundamental laws of heredity as applied to both plants and animals. A basic course for the student concentrating in biological sciences, medicine, allied health fields, agriculture, psychology, or secondary education. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 363L

title: PRINCIPLES OF GENETICS LABORATORY
description: The laboratory course provides the opportunity to apply basic genetic principles in analyses of various experiments. It is designed to both correlate and be a supplement to BIOS 363 . Three hours of lab.

title: HERPETOLOGY
description: A systematic study of the major groups of reptiles and amphibians of the world, including the biology, taxonomy, and ecology of species occurring in the ArkLa-Tex. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 365L

title: HERPETOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Identification of major groups of reptiles and amphibians of the world with emphasis on the species found in the Ark-La-Tex. Field work will involve taxonomy, systematics, and ecology of local species. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 365L

title: ANIMAL BEHAVIOR
description: A study of the observable activity of organisms in response to internal and environmental stimuli. Included will be discussions of the genetic, anatomical, environmental, and evolutionary influences on the behavioral repertoires of motile organisms. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 370L

title: ANIMAL BEHAVIOR LABORATORY
description: The laboratory enables the student to conduct experiments in both the field and laboratory and discuss the behavior patterns observed. Opportunity will be provided for individual work through a required field or laboratory investigation of some behavioral problem. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: BIOS 210 , Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 370

title: PLANT SYSTEMATICS
description: An introduction to floristics and plant systematics with focus on the region, in the context of community ecology, and systematics and taxonomy in general. Skill goals include the relation and evolution, classification, nomenclature and identification of the vascular plants using dichotomous keys and the recognition of key features at the generic and family levels. The class format consists of the lecture, including some hands-on exercises and field excursions; students should be ready to participate in half- or one-day weekend trips in lieu of classroom lectures. Three hours of lecture.

title: PLANT SYSTEMATICS LABORATORY
description: Laboratory study with focus on plant identification using dichotomous keys and other determination methods at the resolution to the generic and family levels, with local species to the species level. Field studies will include various plant communities in Louisiana and the Ark-La-Tex area. Students should be ready to participate in half- or one-day weekend trips in lieu of classroom sessions. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 371

title: COMPARATIVE ANATOMY OF VERTEBRATES
description: A study of vertebrate anatomy based on recent representatives of the living vertebrate classes. Emphasis is on anatomy and anatomical evidence for current concepts of evolutionary relationships of vertebrates. Two hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 372L

title: COMPARATIVE ANATOMY OF VERTEBRATES LABORATORY
description: Individual study and dissection of selected representatives of the recent vertebrate classes. Six hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 372

title: INVERTEBRATE ZOOLOGY
description: Focuses on taxonomy, morphology, physiology, embryology, ecology, and life histories of the invertebrates, protozoa through protochordates. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Twelve hours of biological sciences including BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 374L or consent of the Department

title: INVERTEBRATE ZOOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Laboratory investigation of taxonomy, morphology, physiology, embryology, ecology, and life histories of invertebrates, protozoa through protochordates. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 374

title: VERTEBRATE NATURAL HISTORY
description: A study of the characteristics, life histories, and evolution of representatives of all vertebrate classes. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 376L

title: VERTEBRATE NATURAL HISTORY LABORATORY
description: Field and laboratory study of the characteristics, ecology, and life histories of local species of vertebrates. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 376

title: ANIMAL PARASITOLOGY
description: The general principles of parasitology: morphology, life history, and classification of parasites, and their host relationships. Recommended for students pursuing allied health professions. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 380L

title: ANIMAL PARASITOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Laboratory investigations of the morphology, life history, and classification of parasites with emphasis on techniques of collecting and examining parasites. Three hours of lab
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 380

title: GENERAL PHYSIOLOGY
description: Introduction to the study of homeostatic principles in plants and animals. Integration of chemical and physical processes in biological systems will be presented. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: CHEM 124 , CHEM 124L , and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 387L

title: ORNITHOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Identification of the major groups of birds of the world, including the biology, taxonomy, and ecology, of species occurring in the Ark-La-Tex. Fieldwork will involve the identification, behavior and ecology of local species.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 435

title: PRINCIPLES OF ECOLOGY
description: Fundamental interrelationships between living organisms and the nonliving environments with stress on ecosystem dynamics.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 440L , or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CECO 4123/Principles of Ecology (Upper Level)'}

title: PRINCIPLES OF ECOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Techniques in ecological interpretation and mensuration.
others: {'lab_instruction_hours': 'One hour of lab instruction', 'lab_hours': 'Two hours of lab', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CECO 4121/Principles of Ecology Lab (Upper Level)'}

title: MOLECULAR BASIS OF BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEGENERATION
description: This course covers the molecular and cellular underpinnings of the various stages of brain development. A second part of the course covers the mechanisms underlying degenerative brain diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, Huntington’s disease, prion disease, ALS) and spinomuscular ataxia (SMA). During the semester students will be required to present pub1ications describing important scientific discoveries related to neurodevelopment and neurodegeneration.
prerequisites: BIOS 363 or consent of the department

title: AQUATIC BIOLOGY
description: A study of the physical, chemical, and biological systems of fresh, estuaries, and marine waters.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 446L

title: AQUATIC BIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Practical application of the principles discussed in BIOS 446 with emphasis on fieldwork and identification of the biota of various aquatic habitats.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 446

title: MARINE ECOLOGY
description: Study of the relationships of marine and estuaries organisms to environmental factors, the interactions among organisms, and ecological processes of energy and materials flow. Emphasis will be on the communities and ecosystems of the Louisiana coastal zone.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 , introductory chemistry, and consent of the Department
others: {'semesters_offered': 'Offered summers only at the Louisiana University Marine Consortium (LUMCON) facility'}

title: MARINE MICROBIOLOGY
description: Introduction to estuaries and marine microbes with emphasis on bacteria and fungi. Topics include classification, methodology. Roles in marine ecosystems, biogeochemical cycles and diseases of marine animals.
prerequisites: Twelve term hours of biology and consent of the Department
others: {'semesters_offered': 'Offered summers only at the Louisiana University Marine Consortium (LUMCON) facility'}

title: MARINE BOTANY
description: Study of marine and coastal algae and vascular plants, including classification, morphology, life cycles, and ecology.
prerequisites: Twelve term hours of biology, including some botany, and consent of the Department
others: {'semesters_offered': 'Offered summers only at the Louisiana University Marine Consortium (LUMCON) facility'}

title: COASTAL MARINE GEOLOGY
description: Geomorphologic features of estuaries, coastal and continental shelf environments, erosional, depositional and geochemical processes, and field and laboratory methods.
prerequisites: GEOL 105 and consent of the Department
others: {'semesters_offered': 'Offered summers only at the Louisiana University Marine Consortium (LUMCON) facility'}

title: GENERAL PHYSIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Introduction to laboratory techniques of investigation and observation of physiological functions of plants and animals. One hour of lab instruction and two hours of lab

title: CELL BIOLOGY
description: A study of the structure and function of eukaryotic cells, including ultrastructure, biochemical components, membranes, metabolism and organelles, cytoskeleton, signaling, motility, chromosome structure, and cell divisions. Three hours of lecture.

title: CELL BIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Modern techniques of investigation of cell structure and function, including microscopy, isolation of cell organelles, cytochemistry, and biochemical methodology. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 420

title: IMMUNOLOGY
description: A study of the molecular and cellular processes of immune system function. Topics include innate immunity, recognition of antigen and antigen receptor structure, lymphocyte development, antigen presentation, effector functions, immunodeficiency, allergy, and autoimmune diseases. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 363

title: IMMUNOLOGY LABORATORY
description: An introduction to molecular concepts and techniques used in basic immunology research and in biomedical applications. Includes investigation of the structures of immunoglobulins, T-cell receptors, and major histocompatibility complex molecules. Basic immunological techniques include immunoprecipitation and agglutination, ELISA, hybridoma preparation, immunoaffinity chromatography, and flow cytometry. One hour of lab instruction, and two hours of lab.

title: MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
description: Study of the relationship between the three-dimensional structure and function of proteins and nucleic acids. Topics will include the following: physical methods for the study of macromolecules; protein folding motifs and mechanisms of folding; molecular recognition; DNA topology, replication, repair and recombination; RNA synthesis and processing; genetic code and translation; and molecular mechanisms for regulation of gene expression. Three hours of lecture.

title: MOLECULAR BIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Training in the experimental techniques of molecular biology. Methods taught will include macromolecular purification, electrophoretic analysis, recombinant DNA and cloning techniques, DNA sequencing, polymerase chain reaction, and the use of computers and national databases for the analysis of DNA and protein sequences. One hour of lecture and three hours of lab
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 430

title: HISTOLOGY
description: Microscopic structure and biochemistry of mammalian tissues, stressing relationship of form to function. Three hours of lecture.

title: HISTOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Microscopic structure and biochemistry of mammalian tissues, stressing relationship of form to function. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 434

title: ORNITHOLOGY
description: A systematic study of the major groups of birds of the world, including the biology, taxonomy, and ecology, of species occurring in the Ark-La-Tex. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 222 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 435L

title: MARINE VERTEBRATE ZOOLOGY
description: General study of the marine chordates with particular emphasis on the fishes, including classification, structure, function and ecology. Offered summers only at the Louisiana University Marine Consortium (LUMCON) facility.
prerequisites: Sixteen term hours of biology, including some zoology, and consent of the Department

title: MARINE BIOLOGY
description: An introduction to marine biology, which covers the physical environment, how organisms are influenced by abiotic and biotic factors, different types of marine organisms and habitats, and applied aspects of marine biology.

title: MARINE BIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: An introduction to factors and processes that influence marine organisms and ecosystems through hands-on experience in the lab and field and through computer simulation. Students will engage in data collection, analysis, interpretation, and presentation.
prerequisites: BIOS 210 and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 456

title: MICROBIAL GENETICS
description: Biochemical description of genetic processes in microorganisms. Topics include transformation, conjugation, transduction, replication, recombination, genome organization, gene regulation, and recombinant DNA.
prerequisites: BIOS 363 and one of the following: BIOS 320 , BIOS 420 , or BCHM 410

title: VIROLOGY
description: A study of the structure and function of viruses, including molecular structure, taxonomy, modes of replication, host cell response to viral infections, impact on human health and disease, epidemiology, viral therapeutics, and use of viruses in genetic engineering. Emphasis will be on animal and bacterial viruses.

title: ASTROBIOLOGY
description: A multidisciplinary study of the origin and search for life in the universe. Topics include the chemical origin of life, planetary evolution, search for extra-solar planetary systems and habitable worlds, search for life in the solar system, and search for intelligent life in the universe.

title: MEDICAL GENETICS
description: An integration of the central principles of classical, molecular, and population genetics, with emphasis on their clinical application. This course is intended to provide a comprehensive understanding of Mendelian disorders, cancer genetics, multifactorial inheritance, and clinical cytogenetics and is designed for students preparing for careers in Biological Sciences, medicine, allied health fields, Agriculture, Psychology, or Secondary Education.

title: APPLIED BIOTECHNOLOGY
description: A laboratory based course in which students will gain experience in the principal techniques of recombinant DNA technology.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'six hours of lab per week'}

title: EVOLUTION
description: A study of the theories and mechanisms of evolution.

title: BIOGEOGRAPHY
description: The geographical distributions of major groups of plants and animals will be studied with regard to mechanisms and routes of dispersal, centers of origin, environmental factors, continental drift, and paleontology.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY I
description: Comprehensive overview of the relationship between the chemical structure and biological properties of proteins, lipids and carbohydrates and their contribution to cellar chemistry in terms of enzyme kinetics, bioenergetics, and metabolism. Students are also required to perform critical analyses of assigned biochemical case studies and submit a written report.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY I LABORATORY
description: Experimental investigations on lipid, protein, and carbohydrate biochemistry, including detailed analysis of structural and functional properties of important biomolecules. Students are also required to submit one formal written lab report and make an oral presentation.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'three hours of lab'}

title: BIOCHEMISTRY AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY II
description: Comprehensive overview of the structure, function, and biosynthesis of biomolecules and macromolecular structures. Biochemical descriptions of the genetic apparatus in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells will be emphasized. Students are also required to perform critical analysis of relevant case studies and submit a written report.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY II LABORATORY
description: Isolation and characterization of proteins and nucleic acids using centrifugation, ion exchange and gel permeation chromatography, gel electrophoresis, Spectrophotometry, and enzymology. Students are required to submit one formal written lab report and make an oral presentation.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'three hours of lab'}

title: BIOPHYSICAL CHEMISTRY
description: A comprehensive overview of chemical thermodynamics, kinetics, binding phenomena, and chemical spectroscopy, with an emphasis on the practical applications of modern biological and physical principles. Critical analysis of the modern chemical literature and an oral report are required.

title: EXPERIMENTAL METHODS
description: A study of the processes associated with the scientific method of hypothesis testing. Primary emphasis will be placed on how to formulate hypotheses, design experiments to generate the data necessary to test the hypotheses, use one or a series of instruments and research techniques to generate data, analyze the data statistically, and use the data to draw conclusions related to the initial hypotheses. A variety of state-of-the art research methodologies will be covered.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'six hours of lab per week'}

title: PLANT BIOCHEMISTRY
description: A study of metabolism with emphasis on those metabolic pathways restricted to C3, C4, and CAM photosynthetic plants. Particular emphasis will be placed on the synthesis and breakdown of the major groups of macromolecules as well as secondary plant metabolites. Will also include material regarding the ecological roles of natural plant products.

title: GENERAL BIOLOGY
description: Introduction to general biological principles for the non-science student.

title: GENERAL BIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Supplementary to BIOS 101 by means of student observations, experimentations, and analyses.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 101
others: {'lab_hours': 'Three hours of lab', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CBIO 1011/General Biology I Lab'}

title: MARINE ENVIRONMENT
description: This introductory course in marine biology explores marine organisms, the habitats and physical environment in which they live, and natural and anthropogenic stressors that affect the marine environment. Credit will not be allowed for use in any curricula offered by the Department of Biological Sciences. Designed for the non-science student.
prerequisites: BIOS 101 or consent of the Department

title: FEEDS & FEEDING
description: Basic principles of nutrition including chemical composition of feeding stuffs, digestion, metabolism, functions, and values of nutrients. Two hours of lecture.

title: FEEDS AND FEEDING LABORATORY
description: Computation of balanced rations for farm animals. Two hours of lab.

title: AMER STUDIES SEMINAR
description: An extramural program. Reading conferences, and reports on American history, culture, and values under the direction of a member of the Liberal Arts faculty in Washington, D.C. The subject matter, which is interdisciplinary and broad in scope, will be supplemented by visits to museums, art galleries, theaters, libraries, and other places of cultural and historical interest in Washington, D.C. Three hours of seminar

title: AMER STUDIES SEMINAR
description: An extramural program. Reading conferences, and reports on American history, culture, and values under the direction of a member of the Liberal Arts faculty in Washington, D.C. The subject matter, which is interdisciplinary and broad in scope, will be supplemented by visits to museums, art galleries, theaters, libraries, and other places of cultural and historical interest in Washington, D.C. Three hours of seminar. Same as AMST 198 , except that additional requirements will be appropriate to the level of the course.

title: AMER STUDIES SEMINAR
description: An extramural program. Reading conferences, and reports on American history, culture, and values under the direction of a member of the Liberal Arts faculty in Washington, D.C. The subject matter, which is interdisciplinary and broad in scope, will be supplemented by visits to museums, art galleries, theaters, libraries, and other places of cultural and historical interest in Washington, D.C. Three hours of seminar. Same as AMST 298 , except that additional requirements will be appropriate to the level of the course. Three hours of seminar.

title: THE SOLAR SYSTEM
description: A survey course treating the fundamental principles of the solar system. Three hours of lecture. Louisiana Common Course #: CAST 1103/Astronomy/The Solar System
prerequisites: A sufficiently high score on a math diagnostic test

title: ASTRONOMY LAB
description: Visual and photographic observations of the planets and moon are made with an 8-inch reflecting telescope. In addition, practical problems in solar system astronomy are studied. Two hours of lab.
prerequisites: A laboratory course to accompany ASTR 105

title: STELLAR ASTRONOMY
description: A survey course treating the fundamental principles of stellar astronomy. Three hours of lecture. Louisiana Common Course #: CAST 1113/Astronomy/Stars & Galaxies

title: ASTRONOMY LABORATORY
description: Visual observations of stars and nebulae are made with a reflecting telescope. In addition, practical problems in stellar astronomy are studied. Two hours laboratory.
prerequisites: A laboratory course to accompany ASTR 106

title: INTRODUCTION TO RADIO ASTRONOMY
description: An introduction to the radio universe and the tools and techniques used to explore it. Three hours of lecture/lab

title: ECOSYSTEMS INTERACTIONS
description: Ecosystems Interactions focuses on the interactions of biotic and abiotic factors, particularly climate, soils, and vegetation, which characterize the world’s biomes. An emphasis will be placed on how these factors influence the predictable patterns found in the distribution and characteristics of the world’s biota.
prerequisites: BIOS 224 or consent of the Department

title: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY
description: A study of the molecular mechanisms that regulate development from the zygote to the whole organism in vertebrate and invertebrate animal models. Topics include formation of early body plan, cell type determination, organogenesis, morphogenesis, evolutionary mechanisms, and genetic and environmental contribution to developmental disorders.

title: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Investigation of development using several animal model systems. Topics include gametes and fertilization, cleavage, gastrulation, and morphogenesis of sea urchin, amphibian, and avian embryos.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 475

title: BIOLOGICAL ANTHROPOLOGY
description: An in-depth study of the physical and cultural evolution of non-human primates and humans from a biological perspective. Includes a survey of living and extinct primates. Emphasis will be placed on the origins of genus Homo and the patterns of human variation and adaption.
prerequisites: BIOS 363 or consent of the Department

title: STEM CELL BIOLOGY
description: An in-depth study of the various types of stem cells in the human body and their role in development and disease. Topics covered will include self-renewal, diffrentiation, asymmetric cell division, and diseases that rise from defects in these processes. Current techniqes and technologies will also be covered.

title: ENDOCRINOLOGY
description: The nature of hormones, hormonal mechanisms, feedback processes, and hormonal functions. Some clinical applications will be presented.

title: COMPARATIVE ANIMAL PHYSIOLOGY
description: A comparative study of physiological mechanisms and specialization in animals; ways in which diverse animals perform similar functions.
prerequisites: CHEM 266 , CHEM 266L , BIOS 420 , or consent of the Department and credit or registration for credit in BIOS 485L

title: COMPARATIVE ANIMAL PHYSIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Investigative study of physiological mechanisms of animals.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 485
others: {'lab_hours': 'One hour of lab instruction and two hours of lab'}

title: CLINICAL ANATOMY
description: An overview of the anatomy of the human body using a regional approach, emphasizing anatomy of the thorax, abdomenopelvic cavity, back, head and neck, and upper and lower limbs, while providing information on gross anatomic structures and clinical significance.

title: MEDICAL PHYSIOLOGY
description: The basic concepts of cellular and subcellular structure and function, biological membranes, signal transduction, metabolism, normal physiology of all major organ systems with emphasis on medical application, and pathophysiology of conditions such as shock, heart disease, and renal disease.

title: CLINICAL NEUROANATOMY
description: An overview of the structure and function of the human nervous system, with emphasis on the anatomy of the brain, spinal cord, nerves, and neural pathways, in addition to clinical applications as required by the medical professional.

title: CLINICAL NEUROANATOMY LABORATORY
description: Investigative study of the structures of the human nervous system with emphasis on the gross anatomy of the brain, spinal cord, and nerves. This course supplements the Clinical Neuroanatomy lecture.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN BIOLOGICAL SCIENCES
description: In depth study of special areas in the biological sciences. May be repeated when the topics vary. If the course contains a laboratory component, students must participate in both lecture and lab to earn credit for the course.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One to three hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'zero to four hours of lab'}

title: RESEARCH
description: Laboratory and/or research. Grading in this course is on pass/no credit basis. May be repeated.

title: HONORS THESIS
description: The student must also make a commitment to enroll in BIOS 493 . The student will initiate a research project by selecting a specific area of study, conducting a thorough survey of the literature of the topic and presenting a written outline of the objectives and methods, with a bibliography of the proposed research. The proposed research will not duplicate previously published studies.
prerequisites: Twenty-four hours credit in biological sciences and an invitation from the Department Chair
others: {'conference_hours': 'One hour of conference', 'library_or_lab_hours': 'five hours of library and/or laboratory per week'}

title: HONORS THESIS
description: The student will complete a research project initiated in BIOS 492 . A finished report in the format and style of a manuscript publishable in a professional scientific journal will be submitted to the advisor, reviewed by the student and advisor and revised by the student. The student will present the results and conclusion of his/her research in a style appropriate for a scientific meeting.
others: {'conference_hours': 'One hour of conference', 'lab_hours': 'five hours of lab per week'}

title: ADVANCED FIELD BIOLOGY
description: Advanced training in field techniques required to study the flora and/or fauna and community characteristics of a particular biotic environment. Participants will be expected to work on a group research project as well as specialize in the collection and proper handling and curation of a specific group of organisms. Fieldwork will be completed during a two- or three-week field trip. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: BIOS 291 and consent of the Department

title: SEMINAR
description: An in depth study of a particular topic in biology. One hour of recitation/discussion. Fifty percent (50%) of the grade for the class is derived from the student’s performance on the Biology Major Field Test. The Biology Major Field Test is required of all graduating biology majors. May be repeated for credit two times.
prerequisites: Senior standing, must have credit for, or be currently enrolled in BIOS 363 / BIOS 363L and BIOS 440 / BIOS 440L

title: MEDICAL SEMINAR
description: The medical seminar course features guest speakers from a wide variety of medical disciplines including researchers, practitioners and recruiters from the community who present material representing a wide variety of topics. Course may be taken up to three times for credit. One hour of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 120 and BIOS 120L or consent of the Department

title: CELL BIOLOGY
description: A study of the structure and function of eukaryotic cells, including ultrastructure, biochemical components, membranes, metabolism and organelles, cytoskeleton, signaling, motility, chromosome structure, and cell division. This graduate course will assess critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis.

title: ADVANCED DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY
description: A study of the molecular mechanisms that regulate development from the zygote to the whole organism in vertebrate and invertebrate animal models. Topics include formation of early body plan, cell type determination, organogenesis, morphogenesis, evolutionary mechanisms, and genetic and environmental contribution to developmental disorders. Metacognition will be encouraged and assessed via primary literature readings, problem sets, and leadership of at least one journal club session.

title: BIOLOGICAL ANTHROPOLOGY
description: An in-depth study of the physical and cultural evolution of non-human primates and humans from a biological perspective. Includes a survey of living and extinct primates. Emphasis will be placed on the origins of genus Homo and the patterns of human variation and adaption. This graduate course will assess critical-thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis.

title: CLINICAL ANATOMY
description: An in depth study of the anatomy of the human body using a regional approach, emphasizing anatomy of the thorax, abdominopelvic cavity, back, head and neck, and upper and lower limbs, with emphasis on clinical applications as required by the medical professional. A full body virtual cadaver dissection is included.

title: MEDICAL PHYSIOLOGY
description: An in depth study of cellular and subcellular structure and function, biological membranes, signal transduction, and metabolism in all major organ systems with emphasis on clinical application for the advanced medical professional. Pathophysiology of conditions such as shock, heart disease, renal disease, neuromuscular disease, and diabetes will also be included, with critical analysis of case-based learning modules for these and other pathologies.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN BIOLOGICAL SCIENCES
description: In-depth study of special areas in the biological sciences. May be repeated for credit when the topics vary.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MOLECULAR BIOLOGY I
description: Topics covered include cellular biochemistry, metabolic pathways responsible for energy generation and biosyntheses, protein structure, enzymes, and metabolic regulation.
prerequisites: This course is intended to provide master’s degree-seeking students in System Technology a solid foundation in molecular biology in preparation for their pursuit of a bioinformatics concentration

title: INTRODUCTION TO MOLECULAR BIOLOGY II
description: This course is the second in a molecular biology serious intended to provide master’s degree-seeking students in System Technology a solid foundation in molecular biology in preparation for their pursuit of a bioinformatics concentration. Topics covered include structure and function of the genetic apparatus of the cell, including DNA replication, repair, and recombination, transcription and RNA processing, protein synthesis, gene regulation, and recombinant DNA technology.
prerequisites: BIOS 701 or consent of the Department

title: HISTORY OF SCIENCE
description: Covers the growth of science from ancient Greece to the 20th Century and its impact on the development of western civilization. Emphasis is placed on the biological and chemical sciences.

title: EXPERIMENTAL METHODS
description: A study of the processes associated with the scientific method of hypothesis testing. Primary emphasis will be placed on how to formulate hypotheses, design experiments to generate the data necessary to test the hypotheses, use one or a series of instruments and research techniques to generate data, analyze the data statistically, and use the data to draw conclusions related to the initial hypotheses. A variety of state-of-the art research methodologies will be covered.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture and six hours of lab per week'}

title: BIOLOGY SEMINAR
description: Selected topics from current issues and problems in modern biology based on the primary literature. This course is designed to help the student read, understand, interpret and qualify scientific texts. This course will help train students to find and select essential texts using both literature references 9from an initial text), and computer-based databases and to present the material in an oral and written format.

prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 620

description: Microscopic structure and biochemistry of mammalian tissues, stressing relationship of form to function and histological techniques.

title: ORNITHOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Identification of the major groups of birds of the world, including the biology, taxonomy, and ecology or species occurring in the Ark-La-Tex. Fieldwork will involve the identification, behavior, and ecology of local species. This graduate course will assess critical thinking skills through scholarly readings, written analysis, and a filed research project of an ecological or behavioral nature.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 635

title: PRINCIPLES OF ECOLOGY
description: Rigorous background in population and community analyses covering growth and regulation, species interactions, life-history theory, and viability analysis including process-based modeling of biological systems.

title: PRINCIPLES OF ECOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Exploration of applied and quantitative methods to explore biotic and abiotic patterns in populations, communities and ecosystems.

title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
description: Introduction to laboratory and library research. Three hours per week

title: CHEMISTRY LITERATURE
description: Use of chemical literature. One hour of lecture.

title: PHYSICAL CHEMISTRY I
description: Introduction to thermodynamics, and kinetic theory of gases. Three hours lecture and two hours recitation.

title: PHYSICAL CHEMISTRY I LABORATORY
description: Selected experiments in thermodynamics. Three hours of lab instruction.

title: PHYSICAL CHEMISTRY II
description: Chemical kinetics and the introduction to the theories of atomic and molecular structure. Three hours of lecture and two hours of recitation.

title: PHYSICAL CHEMISTRY II LABORATORY
description: Selected experiments in kinetics, thermodynamics, atomic and molecular structure. Three hours of lab instruction.

title: STRUCTURE ELUCIDATION OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS
description: Structure elucidation of organic molecules by chemical and instrumental methods. One hour of instruction and five hours of lab.

title: DESCRIPTIVE INORGANIC CHEMISTRY
description: Basic descriptive inorganic chemistry dealing in a systematic way with the elements and the structures, properties and reactions of their inorganic compounds. Topics range from coordination chemistry to organometallic chemistry to bioinorganic chemistry. Three hours of lecture.

title: INTRODUCTION TO CHEMICAL PHYSICS
description: A continuation of CHEM 304 . Three hours of lecture

title: PRINCIPLES OF ENVIRONMENTAL CHEMISTRY
description: Principles of Chemistry and chemical technology pertinent to the environment. Topics include air and water quality; toxic, agricultural, and domestic chemicals; energy sources; waste disposal; and nuclear chemistry. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: CHEM 255 and CHEM 266 , PHYS 252 , and three hours of Biology, or consent of the Department

title: HEALTH SCIENCE CAPSTONE
description: The Health Sciences Capstone course is specific for students in the Health Sciences concentration to review topics in biochemistry, behavioral sciences, anatomy, physiology, microbiology, pathology, pharmacology, and interdisciplinary topics.
prerequisites: Credit or registration for credit in BIOS 686

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN BIOLOGY
description: Special topics in Biology, which may be repeated as topics, vary from term to term

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY IN BIOLOGY
description: Independent study in biology. An independent project or study directed by an assigned faculty member

title: CAPSTONE
description: Completion, presentation, and successful defense of an independent research project for non-thesis students in the Master of Science in Biological Sciences program. Projects will be approved by the graduate program and products can embody a variety of formats. Must be taken in the final semester of the non-thesis option.
others: {'grading': 'Grading on this course is a pass/no credit basis.'}

title: THESIS
description: Thesis. Research project carried out under the supervision of assigned faculty member.
others: {'grading': 'Grading on this course is a pass/no credit basis.', 'repeatability': 'May be repeated.'}

title: PERSONAL LAW
description: A practical course designed to help individuals gain better awareness and understanding of basic legal problems encountered in modern society. Topics covered include landlord-tenant relations, buying/selling real estate, civil procedure, torts, simple contracts, family law, students’ rights, and probate law.

title: LEGAL ENVIRONMENT OF BUSINESS
description: Structure and function of the legal institutions in society and their relationship to business institutions including the following areas: Consumer protection, anti-trust, corporation reporting and securities, environment, employment, and ethics.

title: REAL ESTATE LAW
description: Legal problems incident to ownership and transfer of real property, with special emphasis on Louisiana law.

title: BUSINESS LAW I
description: Study of contracts, property, agency and the basic legal principles of business organizations and operations, including practical comparison and assessment of the different kinds of business organizations.

title: BUSINESS LAW II
description: Legal concepts and practices concerning the laws of the commercial transaction. Topics covered include sales and credit contracts, negotiable instruments, secured transactions, suretyship, insurance, and bankruptcy.

title: MOLECULAR BASIS OF BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEGENERATION
description: This course covers the molecular and cellular underpinnings of the various stages of brain development. A second part of the course covers the mechanisms underlying degenerative brain diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, Huntington’s disease, prion disease, ALS and spinomuscular ataxia (SMA). During the semester students will be required to present publications describing important scientific discoveries related to neurodevelopment and neurodegeneration.

title: AQUATIC BIOLOGY
description: A critical study of physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of freshwater and inland aquatic ecosystems, addressing both natural processes and anthropogenic impacts. Topics to be covered include characteristics of different types of inland bodies of water, typical organisms found in them, and factors responsible for influencing community structure and ecosystem function.

title: AQUATIC BIOLOGY LABORATORY
description: Practical application of the principles of aquatic biology with emphasis on the critical analysis of field work, the identification of the biota found in aquatic habitats, techniques and equipment used in sample collection, and data collection, analysis, and presentation.

title: MICROBIAL GENETICS
description: Biochemical description of genetic processes in microorganisms. Topics include transformation, conjugation, transduction, replication, recombination, genome organization, gene regulation, and recombinant DNA. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis.

title: VIROLOGY
description: A study of the structure and function of viruses, including molecular structure, taxonomy, modes of replication, host cell response to viral infections, impact on human health and disease, epidemiology, viral therapeutics, and use of viruses in genetic engineering. Emphasis will be on animal and bacterial viruses. This graduate course will assess critical-thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis.

title: ASTROBIOLOGY
description: A multidisciplinary study of the origin and search for life in the universe. Tpoics include the chemical origin of life, planetary evolution, search for extra-solar planetary systems and habitable worlds, search for life in the solar system, and search for intelligent life in the universe. This graduate course will assess critical thinking skills through scholary readings and written analsys.

title: MEDICAL GENETICS
description: An integration of the central principles of classical, molecular, and population genetics with their clinical application to genetic disorder. An advanced course for the graduate student concentrating in Biological Sciences, medicine, allied health fields, Psychology, or Secondary Education. Critical thinking skills and problem solving exercises will be assessed through case studies, analysis, and review of primary literature.

title: APPLIED BIOTECHNOLOGY
description: A laboratory based course that will enable students to gain experience in the basic techniques of molecular biology. The graduate course also requires students to demonstrate their ability to write a grant proposal describing molecular biology investigations.

title: EVOLUTION
description: A study of the theories and mechanisms of organic evolution. This course will have particular value for those students who wish to pursue a graduate degree in Biological Science Education.

title: BIOGEOGRAPHY
description: The geographic distribution of major groups of plants and animals will be studied with regard to mechanisms and routes of dispersal centers of origin, environmental factors, continental drift, and paleontology. This course will have particular value for those students who wish to pursue a graduate degree in Biological Science Education.

title: ECOLOGICAL METHODOLOGY
description: BIOS 740 will emphasize the methodology for estimating abundance in animal and plant populations, sampling and experimental design, estimating community parameters, and life tables and survivorship estimations. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: BIOS 440 or equivalent or consent of the department

title: PLANT MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
description: The impact of genetic engineering on agriculture. Methods of transformation, antisense technology, gene regulation, plant tissue culture, the chloroplast genome, transposable elements, and the prospects for crop improvement. The course will include the critical reading of relevant research papers. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Previous course work in plant physiology, biochemistry and molecular biology

title: ECOTOXICOLOGY
description: A study of the chemical properties, mode of action, detection and environmental fate of the major pesticides. Three hours of lecture

title: MARINE SCIENCE FOR TEACHERS
description: Survey of the marine sciences, field and classroom techniques for the teaching of marine science at secondary school levels.
others: {'offered': 'summers only at the Louisiana University Marine Consortium (LUMCON) facility'}

title: HUMAN GENETICS FOR TEACHERS
description: Study of human inheritance patterns, genetic disorders, genetic screening and counseling, social impact of genetics, and current research. Three hours of lecture.

title: ADVANCED CELL AND MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
description: This is an advanced cell and molecular biology course that that focuses on four to five major topics, such as signal transduction pathways, intracellular organization and protein sorting, intracellular transport, cell cycle regulation and regulated cell death. Three hours of lecture

title: SELECTED TOPICS IN BIOLOGY
description: May be repeated for credit when the topics vary. Special topics to be selected from areas such as botany, genetics, microbiology, or zoology. Three hours of lecture.

title: INDEPENDENT RESEARCH
description: Library, laboratory, or field research under the guidance of a Departmental advisor. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of research per credit.

title: SCIENTIFIC COMMUNICATION
description: This course will introduce graduate students to fundamental concepts for effective scientific communication via oral presentations and writing: manuscripts, grant proposals, and patent disclosures.

title: BIOSTATISTICS
description: Basic concepts of statistical models and sampling methods, descriptive statistical measures, distributions, tests of significance, analysis of variance, regression, correlation, and chi-square; emphasis on field-oriented life and environmental sciences research problems; computer software applications. Three hours of lecture.

title: GENERAL CHEMISTRY I
description: A fundamental course in chemistry including atomic structure, chemical bonding, nomenclature, stoichiometry, chemical reactions, thermochemistry, electronic configurations, molecular geometry, molecular orbitals, and gases. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MATH 121 (or higher); it is also strongly suggested that students should have completed a course in high school chemistry or CHEM 110

title: GENERAL CHEMISTRY LABORATORY I
description: A laboratory course in fundamental chemical operations including density, stoichiometry, heat laws, gas laws, molecular weights, and basic chemical reactivity. One hour of instruction and two hours of lab.

title: GENERAL CHEMISTRY II
description: Solution chemistry, solids, liquids, kinetics, equilibrium, thermodynamics, electrochemistry, nuclear chemistry, and coordination compounds. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: CHEM 121 or CHEM 110 , with consent of the Department

title: GENERAL CHEMISTRY LABORATORY II
description: Experimental techniques dealing with kinetics, equilibrium, solubility, electrochemistry, acids and bases, complexation, and qualitative analysis of ions. One hour of instruction and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: CHEM 121L and credit or registration in CHEM 124
others: {'lccn': 'CCEM 1131/Chemistry II Lab (Science Majors)', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CCEM 1131/Chemistry II Lab (Science Majors)'}

title: ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY
description: Basic principles and practices of modern methods of analysis. Three hours of lecture.

title: ANALYTICAL CHEMISTY LABORATORY
description: Experiments in modern methods of analysis. Three hours of lab.
prerequisites: CHEM 124L and CHEM 255 or concurrent enrollment in CHEM 255

title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY I
description: A fundamental course for majors in pre-medicine, biological sciences, or chemistry. Structure, nomenclature, chemical properties, and physical properties (including UV, lR, and NMR) of selected 243 classes of organic compounds. Reaction mechanisms emphasis along with correlation and identification of these compounds. Three hours of lecture.

title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY LABORATORY I
description: An introduction to the laboratory techniques of separation, purification, and identification of organic compounds and to organic chemical synthesis. One hour of lecture and two hours of lab work.
prerequisites: CHEM 124L and credit or registration in CHEM 265

title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY II
description: Optical isomerism and heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen, and/or oxygen, and/or sulfur and to a lesser degree, compounds containing phosphorus or silicon. Three hours of lecture.

title: ORGANIC CHEMISTRY LABORATORY II
description: A continuation of CHEM 265L with an emphasis on identification techniques that include structure elucidation using spectroscopy and on organic chemical synthesis. One hour of lecture and two hours of lab work.
prerequisites: CHEM 265L and credit or registration in CHEM 266

title: MATERIALS SCIENCE
description: Topics studied include polymer chemistry, solid-state chemistry, crystallography, and chemistry of modern materials. Three hours of lecture.

title: INSTRUMENTAL ANALYSIS
description: Modern instrumental analysis. Three hours of lecture.

title: INSTRUMENTAL ANALYSIS LABORATORY
