Each student will write a research paper on an approved topic and present it. Three hours of combined lecture and lab in a computer lab setting.

title: WEB DESIGN I
description: An introduction to creating and managing websites. Course includes introduction to hypertext markup language (HTML) and cascading style sheets (CSS). Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab

title: GAME DESIGN I
description: Introduction to concepts associated with game theory and developing visual assets for video games. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.

title: 3D PRINTING I
description: An introduction to the pipelines and workflows used in the current 3D printing industry, with a focus on the creative arts. An overview of the modeling approaches using 3D modeling and scanning software, and final Printing Resources and postproduction techniques will be covered. This course assumes you have no prior experience with 3D Modeling software. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: Digital Arts major or consent of the Department

title: CHARACTER DESIGN
description: An introduction to the art of creating original characters for 2D and 3D animation. The course will cover topics including, but not limited to, how to draw and develop visual characters, both professional and personal drawing and construction techniques, and how to convert an idea into finished form. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: Digital Arts major or consent of the Department

title: 3D MODELING I
description: Introduction to basic 3D modeling and texturing. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.

title: DIGITAL SCULPTING
description: Concepts, processes, and techniques present in 3- dimensional digital sculpting. The student will be introduced to a workflow involving model creation using industry standard software packages. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: Digital Arts major or consent of the Department

title: 3D ANIMATION I
description: Introduction to basic 3D animation. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.

title: DIGITAL PAINTING
description: Introduction to digital painting with an emphasis on artistic technique. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: FA 200 or consent of the Department

title: CORPORATE IDENTITY DESIGN
description: Introduction to corporate identity systems and branding as well as the development and design of promotional assets for use in advertising campaigns. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: DA 211 , DA 315 , and DA 318 , or consent of the Department

title: ADVERTISING CAMPAIGN
description: Topics designed to further enhance student understanding and development of promotional assets with an emphasis on creating a complete advertising campaign. Will include group activities. Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: DA 412 or consent of the Department. Continuation of DA 412

title: INTRODUCTION TO COMPETITIVE DEBATE
description: Students will be introduced to competitive debate through practice and/or competitive travel participating in tournaments held at other colleges and universities. Students gain an introductory knowledge in skills such as research, organization, critical thinking, delivery, refutation, and cross-examination. May be repeated for a maximum of three hours. One hour of lecture.

title: INTERMEDIATE COMPETITIVE DEBATE
description: Students will continue their experience in competitive academic debate through practice and/or competitive travel participating in tournaments held at other colleges and universities. Students gain an intermediate knowledge in skills such as research, organization, critical thinking, delivery, refutation, and cross-examination. Students will also serve as peer coaching mentors to DEB 165 novice students. May not be taken simultaneously with DEB 165 or DEB 365 . May be repeated for a maximum of three hours. One hour of lecture.
prerequisites: DEB 165 or consent of the Director of Debate

title: ADVANCED COMPETITIVE DEBATE
description: Students will continue their experience in competitive academic debate through practice and/or competitive travel participating in tournaments held at other colleges and universities. Students gain an advanced knowledge in skills such as research, organization, critical thinking, delivery, refutation, and cross-examination. Students will also serve as peer coaching mentors to DEB 165 and DEB 265 students. May not be taken simultaneously with DEB 165 or DEB 265 . May be repeated for a maximum of three hours. One hour of lecture.
prerequisites: DEB 165 and DEB 265 or consent of the Director of Debate

title: DISASTER PREPAREDNESS ADMINISTRATION
description: An exploration of administration approaches to preparing for, responding to, recovering from, and mitigating natural or terrorist-initiated events. This is an online course
prerequisites: MADM 301 , POLI 201 , SOCL 302 , or consent of the Department

title: LOCAL, STATE, AND NATIONAL DISASTER RESPONSE
description: An exploration of the different expectations for disaster administration at the local, state, and national levels, as well as the complexity of policy issues impinging on preparedness initiatives. This is an online course.

title: VOLUNTEER ADMINISTRATION FOR DISASTER RESPONSE
description: An overview of volunteer management principles and practices with a focus on the role that volunteers have in disaster response. Jurisdictional issues, communications, legal risks, and other factors influencing volunteer use will be examined. This is an online course.

title: 3D MODELING II
description: Continuation of DA 330 with an emphasis on creating a fully textured model.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: DISASTER PREPAREDNESS ADMINISTRATION
description: An exploration of the theory, research, government policy, and guidelines preparing for, responding to, recovering from, and mitigating natural or terrorist-initiated events. Particular attention will be devoted to analysis of differences between similar type disasters at various times in history, with student projects based on original research leading to a critique of the response to, recover from, and mitigation of the disaster and operational suggestions for the future. This is an online course.

title: 3D ANIMATION II
description: Continuation of DA 332 with emphasis on creating a fully animated model.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: LOCAL, STATE, AND NATIONAL DISASTER RESPONSE
description: An exploration of the different expectations for disaster administration at the local, state, and national levels, focusing on how these have changed over time, as well as on the complexity of policy issues impinging on preparedness initiatives. Students will conduct original research projects that synthesize literature and propose more effective coordination and response. This is an online course.

title: ENVIRONMENT MODELING
description: An introduction to the pipelines and work-flows used to create 3D environments and props in the commercial, entertainment, and gaming industries. An overview of the modeling approaches using 3D modeling with various levels of detail (LOD), along with appropriate texture map creation for use in the various work-flows will be covered in depth, along with personal and in-class project exploration.
prerequisites: DA 330 , DA 331 , or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: VOLUNTEER ADMINISTRATION FOR DISASTER RESPONSE
description: Students will conduct original research into past responses of volunteer organizations to disasters. They will evaluate these responses in light of current management theory and will produce a paper aimed at presentation at a professional conference or publication in a professional journal.

title: MATERIALS AND LIGHTING
description: Designed for students with some experience in 3D Modeling, the course will cover how to prepare 3D models for texturing and mapping in order to create unique surface materials.
prerequisites: DA 330 or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: PRINCIPLES OF MACROECONOMICS
description: An introductory study of the nature of macroeconomics, with emphasis on national income determination and the monetary system. Three hours of lecture.

title: HISTORY OF MEDIA ARTS
description: An introductory survey of the history of graphic design and digital media including the history of media arts, its methods, and major digital trends in their social and historical contexts.

title: SEMINAR IN DIGITAL ARTS
description: Advanced topics in digital arts. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours of credit.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: DESIGN FOR SOCIAL IMPACT
description: An introduction to applying graphic design skills and design thinking methods to address societal needs via human-centered co-design. Students will be given the unique opportunity to create tangible solutions for real design problems. Students will work collaboratively and actively with stakeholders, research, ideate possible solutions, apply feedback, and present thoughtfully developed deliverables.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY IN DIGITAL ARTS
description: Emphasis on expressive independent works of digital art. The student will complete a major project in an area of specialty with faculty guidance. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours of credit.

title: SENIOR CAPSTONE
description: Students will present a professional portfolio, thesis, gallery show, or other appropriate work. A copy of the finished work will remain with the Department for assessment purposes.
prerequisites: Senior standing or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: INTERNSHIP
description: Provides an opportunity for experience directly under the supervision of a recognized area fine arts professional. The student must not have previously worked for the agency sponsoring the internship. Minimum of 120 hours of work required. Courses will be graded on a pass/no credit basis.
prerequisites: Digital Arts major, 2.75 overall GPA, and 3.2 Digital Arts GPA

title: PRINC OF MICROECONOMICS
description: An introductory study of the nature of microeconomics, with emphasis on consumption, production, and resource allocation from a private and social point of view.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF ECONOMICS
description: An introductory study of the principles of macroeconomics and microeconomics. The course is designed to give the student a background in essential economic theory necessary to satisfy the MBA foundations requirements. Students will attend lectures as well as complete self-directed computerized instructional materials. Credit will not be allowed for undergraduate business majors.

title: AGGREGATE ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
description: Analysis of the factors determining aggregate national income, employment and unemployment, capital accumulation and economic growth, business cycles, and analysis of the monetary system.

title: INTERMEDIATE ECONOMIC THEORY
description: The study of resource allocation and factor pricing in an enterprise economy. Some consideration is given to the application of microeconomic theory in business decision-making.

title: MONEY, BANKING AND MONETARY POLICY
description: An intermediate study of the nature and functions of money, the banking system of the United States, and monetary theory.
others: {'louisiana_common_course': 'CECN 3113/Money, Banking, and the Economy (Upper Level)', 'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture'}

title: HISTORY OF ECONOMIC THOUGHT
description: Study of the development of economic thought, and how those ideas influence business, politics, and society. Discussion of the major schools of thought and how their tenets (correct or erroneous) perpetuate today and influence current public policy.

title: INTERNATIONAL ECONOMICS
description: An introduction to the theories of trade, international payments, foreign exchange rates, instruments, markets, and adjustment of international disequilibria.

title: SEMINAR IN ECONOMICS
description: Selected topics will vary from term to term. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours.
prerequisites: Junior standing and consent of the Department

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY IN ECONOMICS
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the guidance of a member of the economics faculty. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six credit hours, provided the topics differ.

title: FUNDAMENTALS OF ECONOMICS
description: An introduction to the foundations of economic analysis.
others: {'grading': 'Grading will be on a pass/fail basis'}

title: SCHOOL COUNSELING PRACTICUM
description: Supervised experience in working with children and youth in a school guidance program. A minimum of 100 hours of practical experience will be required. Three hours of lecture/lab.

title: INTRODUCTION TO DUAL ENROLLMENT
description: Explores the history, principles, and philosophy of Dual Enrollment, with a focus on successes and documented challenges across a variety of Dual Enrollment models. Addresses differences between high-school and university-level courses and issues of diversity, equity, and inclusion, and how these are negotiated through college-level coure design, syllabus building, and expectation of students.

title: SURVEY OF PEDAGOGY
description: Introduces students to history of pedagogy in specific academic disciplines, with a focus on best practices in college-level teaching based on current research in the student’s discipline.

title: EDUCATIONAL TECHNOLOGIES
description: A comprehensive introduction to educational technologies used by post-secondary institutions, with a focus on those currently used at consortium institutions, such as learning management systems, library databases, AI, third-party vendor software used by various departments, and interfaces used by the Student Success Center. Students will be asked to engage critically with these technologies using discipline-specific materials, and, more broadly, with the role and philosophy of educational technology adoption and use.

title: TEACHING PRACTICUM
description: A practicum that places the student in the college classroom, moving from observation and reflection, to delivery of existing modules, to creation and delivery of independent modules. Includes implementation of appropriate technologies, assignment design, grading.

title: INTRODUCTION TO FOUNDATIONS OF EDUCATION
description: Lecture, reading, and discussion designed to introduce the student to the foundations of the teaching profession.

title: INTRODUCTION TO TECHNOLOGY IN EDUCATION
description: A study of applications of educational technology for the classroom, with attention to selection of hardware and software, use of audiovisual media, and development of instructional materials.

title: INTRODUCTION TO SPECIAL EDUCATION
description: This course is a general introduction to the characteristics and needs of exceptional learners and their education. Emphasis is on classroom practices, as well as the psychological, sociological, and medical aspects of exceptionalities.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY IN EDUCATION
description: Reading, on-site visits to schools and other institutions, conferences, and reports under the guidance of an education faculty member. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. One hour of seminar and three hours of research.
prerequisites: ED 201 or consent of the Department
others: {'seminar_hours': 'One hour of seminar', 'research_hours': 'three hours of research.'}

title: TEACHING BASIC SUBJECTS TO CHILDREN WITH SPECIAL NEEDS
description: Methods, materials, and differentiation for instruction in the basic academic subjects for pupils with mild and moderate levels of special needs. Candidates are required to apply knowledge and skills in field-based experiences.

title: COMPUTER GRAPHICS
description: Techniques for representation, transformation, and display of patterns and images on graphics display devices. Students will conduct a thorough independent analysis of a current computer graphics topic.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture in a computer lab setting'}

title: RAPID APPLICATIONS DEVELOPMENT
description: A study of tools and techniques used in the rapid development of applications that run in current operating system environments. The object-oriented and visual programming paradigms will be emphasized. Students will complete an independent RAD project.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture in a computer lab setting'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO BIOINFORMATICS
description: Introduces principles, concepts, methods, techniques, algorithms, tools, and strategies to transform and process the masses of information from biological experiments. Focusing computational methods to analyze genome and protein sequences to derive structural and functional information. Student teams conceive, design, specify, implement, evaluate, and report on a software project in the domain of biomedicine.

title: INFORMATION VISUALIZATION
description: An in-depth examination of computer-based strategies for interactive visual presentation of information to explore, discover, and learn from large data sets. Study of classic and advanced computer visualization principles, techniques, and tools used for explaining and understanding symbolic, structured, and/or hierarchical information. Projects will apply design principles, interaction strategies, information types, and experimental results.

title: INTORDUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
description: This course covers an introductory study of fundamental Artificial Intelligence (AI) topics, including agents, search, constraint satisfaction, game theory, logic, reasoning under uncertainty, decision theory, Markov decision processes, Bayesian learning, and reinforcement learning. Topics include techniques and algorithms from AI to tackle complex real-world problems, such as web search, speech recognition, face or object recognition, machine translation, fraud detection, autonomous driving, and medical diagnosis. Student teams design, implement, and evaluate an approved project on one of the domains of AI and present it.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture in a computer lab setting'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO MACHINE LEARNING
description: Knowledge of Python and statistics desirable. Computer programs that can automatically improve performance through experience. Linear models of classification and regression; supervised and unsupervised learning; kernel methods; sequential models; neural networks and introduction to deep learning. Recent applications in speech recognition and computer vision.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture in a classroom/computer lab setting'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO DATA MINING
description: Introduction to the fundamental and advanced concepts of data mining and knowledge discovery. Learn to analyze, design, develop and evaluate techniques and tools. Topics include data preprocessing, data characterization and comparison, decision trees, association rule mining (also in large databases), classification and prediction, clustering and cluster analysis and statistical modeling.

title: DEEP LEARNING
description: Artificial neural networks with multiple layers and richer feature learning. Convolutional Neural Network; Recurrent Neural Network; Long Short-Term Memory; Auto-encoder; Generative Adversarial Network. Representative learning. Applications and recent advances.
prerequisites: Consent of the Department. Python required
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture in a classroom/computer lab setting'}

title: MOBILE ROBOTICS
description: The course introduces the design and implementation of different types of robots, sensors, and locomotion. Topics include algorithms and models for control, perception, sensing, localization, mapping, planning, and navigation. Students taking this class benefit from mastering the basic aspects of robotics and from implementing algorithms for different aerial, mobile, and manipulator robots. Student projects involve the literature study of a fundamental robotic task and develop a solution to the task in real-world settings.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture in a computer lab setting'}

title: INTERNSHIP IN COMPUTER SCIENCE
description: Employment-focused learning with a qualified employer. The course requires written reports detailing the objectives, progress, and completion of the internship. Three hours of credit on a Pass/No Credit basis. May be repeated once.

title: PRACTICUM IN READING
description: A study of different approaches to the teaching of reading, knowledge and dispositions of reading and writing processes, instructional practices, methods, and materials to support reading and writing instruction, and the use of assessment tools to plan instruction. Participation in an elementary classroom is required. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: ED 318 Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN SECONDARY SCHOOL READING
description: Materials and methods used in teaching reading in the content areas in the secondary school. Participation in a secondary classroom is required. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: DYSLEXIA FOR EDUCATORS
description: An overview of the body of scientific work regarding dyslexia, including the history, epidemiology, and clinical presentation, including early clinical indicators of dyslexia and common, persistent classroom presentations. An overview of evidence-based instruction for individuals with dyslexia including remediation of weaknesses, fortification of strengths, and common accommodations to help mediate between the two. An introduction to the process of becoming a dyslexia practitioner or dyslexia therapist.
prerequisites: ED 316 and ED 317 or ED 320 or permission of the Department.

title: ASSESSMENT AND EVALUATION IN INCLUSIVE EDUCATION
description: This course is designed for teacher candidates to learn how to select, adapt, and use instructional interventions and behavioral strategies with students in a variety of settings. Candidates are required to apply knowledge and skills in field-based experiences. Three hours of lecture

title: SECONDARY MATHEMATICS PRAXIS II PREPARATION
description: Secondary Mathematics PRAXIS II will prepare Secondary Education Mathematics majors for the Praxis II Mathematics Content Exam, which is required prior to beginning Residency I.
prerequisites: Semester before Praxis scores due and consent of Department

title: TEACHING VOCATIONAL EDUCATION TO CHILDREN WITH SPECIAL NEEDS
description: Methods, materials, and differentiation for instruction in the basic academic subjects for pupils with mild and moderate levels of special needs. Candidates are required to apply knowledge and skills in field-based experiences. Three hours of lecture.

title: PREPARATION FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE IN THE ELEMENTARY SCHOOL
description: Supervised experience in planning, directing, and evaluating learning experiences in elementary education. Two hours of seminar and three hours of lab. Grading is pass/no credit.
prerequisites: Passing PRAXIS Content and PLT Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: CLASSROOM MANAGEMENT IN THE ELEMENTARY SCHOOL
description: A study of classroom management techniques and research related to effective management of classroom learning environments, student behaviors, and classroom resources in the secondary school. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Must be taken concurrently with ED 422 or ED 424 Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MIDDLE SCHOOL
description: A study of principles, practices, trends and issues in curriculum and instruction in schools serving early adolescent learners. Participation in a secondary classroom is required. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: CLASSROOM MANAGEMENT IN THE SECONDARY SCHOOL
description: A study of classroom management techniques and research related to effective management of classroom learning environments, student behaviors, and classroom resources in the secondary school. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.
corequisites: Must be taken concurrently with ED 422 or ED 424

title: CLASSROOM MOTIVATION
description: An introduction to methods of management that emphasizes the interpersonal nature of the educational process, motivation, and discipline apart from those considered behavioristic in nature.

title: COLLABORATION FOR INCLUSIVE EDUCATION
description: This course will introduce strategies that will develop effective partnership and collaboration between education professionals, parents, and students.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS OF EARLY CHILDHOOD MATHEMATICS
description: Theoretical bases and practical application of the early childhood mathematics curriculum, including scope and sequence of skills, organization of instruction, teaching strategies, and evaluation and assessment techniques. Participation in an early childhood classroom is required.

title: METHODS AND MATERIALS IN ELEMENTARY MATH I
description: Theoretical bases and practical application of the lower elementary mathematics curriculum, including scope and sequence of skills, organization of instruction, teaching strategies, and evaluation and assessment techniques that meet the needs of diverse learners. Participation in an elementary classroom is required.
prerequisites: MATH 117 (ECE majors and Elementary Education majors). Credit for or concurrent enrollment in MATH 217 (Elementary Education majors only) Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: METHODS AND MATERIALS IN ELEMENTARY MATH II
description: Theoretical bases and practical application of the upper elementary mathematics curriculum, including scope and sequence of skills, organization of instruction, teaching strategies, and evaluation and assessment techniques. Participation in an elementary classroom is required.
prerequisites: Students must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN ELEMENTARY SOCIAL STUDIES AND SCIENCE
description: Materials and methods used in teaching social studies and science in the elementary grades. Course restricted to Elementary Alternative Certification students.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN ELEMENTARY SCHOOL MATH
description: Materials and methods in teaching mathematics in the elementary school.
prerequisites: ED 201 , ED 202 , PSYC 206 , and grades of C or better in MATH 117 and MATH 217 . Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN ELEMENTARY LANGUAGE ARTS
description: Theoretical and practical bases of teaching language arts in the elementary classroom, to include language development, grammar, spelling, handwriting, process writing, and oral language activities. Participation in an elementary classroom is required.

title: READING/WRITING PROCESSES I
description: Reading and writing development of the primary elementary student. Emphasis on foundational knowledge, dispositions, assessment, instructional strategies, and curriculum materials. Participation in an elementary classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: READING/WRITING PROCESSES II
description: The study of foundational knowledge, dispositions, assessment, instructional strategies, and curriculum materials used to develop elementary readers and writers. Participation in an elementary classroom is required.
prerequisites: ED 317 Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: LEARNING AND EDUCATION
description: This course focuses on theories on how human learning occurs, with particular emphasis on their application in K16 school settings. After reviewing background on the definition and purposes of learning theories, students study origins, features, and applications of various theories, including: behavioral, information processing, social 272 cognitive, developmental, and constructivist. Course activities help students explore how learning theories shape educational practices in schools and identify those theories that most influence their own teaching practices.

title: MENTOR TEACHER TRAINING
description: The identification, modification and use of high quality instructional materials for use by diverse learners and the creation of text sets. Diagnose and prioritize areas for growth, provide coaching and support, monitor progress, and adjust mentoring as needed to improve content instruction and classroom management.

title: CURRICULUM
description: A systematic study of the history, theory and practice of curriculum development in American education.

title: ADVANCED TOPICS IN MATH EDUCATION
description: A study of curriculum in school mathematics and instructional strategies with emphasis on diagnosis and remediation.

title: ADVANCED TOPICS IN TEACHING ENGLISH
description: A study of theory and strategies for teaching English. Designed around major topics of concern to certified teachers.

title: ADVANCED TOPICS IN SOCIAL STUDIES
description: An analysis of curricular issues, teaching practices, and social influences concerning modern social studies education.

title: EDUCATIONAL SUPERVISORY PRACTICE
description: A study of principles and techniques used in supervising and mentoring preservice teacher candidates and inservice teachers.

title: CREATING MULTICULTURAL CONNECTIONS THROUGH LITERATURE
description: This course emphasizes a critical approach to examination and selection of literature for K-16 school settings. Review and analysis of theories establish a foundation upon which to critique examples of literature, to view personal practice, and to adjust instructional approaches. Candidates will explore and identify literature selections to generate a stronger connection for all learners.

title: FOUNDATIONS IN READING
description: Study of the theories and models of reading and the various instructional approaches to the teaching of reading. Provides an overview of the reading process from emergent literacy through adulthood. Includes practical training in vocabulary and comprehension development.

title: READING ASSESSMENT
description: Study of formal and informal diagnostic tests and techniques used in assessing reading ability with emphasis on organizing and implementing assessment based instruction.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN SECONDARY SCIENCES
description: Curriculum and methods in teaching sciences in secondary schools with an emphasis on lesson plan writing and demonstration lessons. Participation in a secondary classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN SECONDARY ENGLISH
description: Curriculum and methods in teaching English in secondary schools with an emphasis on lesson plan writing and demonstration lessons. Participation in a secondary classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN SECONDARY MATHEMATICS
description: Curriculum and methods in teaching Mathematics in secondary schools with an emphasis on lesson plan writing and demonstration lessons. Participation in a secondary classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN SECONDARY SOCIAL STUDIES
description: Curriculum and methods in teaching Social Studies in secondary schools with an emphasis on lesson plan writing and demonstration lessons. Participation in a secondary classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT OF MULTIMEDIA INSTRUCTIONAL UNITS
description: Topics include instructional design and multimedia development, multimedia design principles, step-by-step design and development process, along with hands-on activities in instructional multimedia tools.

title: EDUCATIONAL TELECOMMUNICATIONS, NETWORKS, AND THE INTERNET
description: Hands-on activities using the World Wide Web to plan, develop, and manage distance learning, networking, and internet use in the workplace.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN EDUCATION
description: Current and/or special issues in education will vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

description: Hands-on activities that allow the use of resources and the World Wide Web which include the planning, development, implementation, and management of distance learning, networking, and internet use in the workplace. This course assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis of a more complex nature than is true for the undergraduate equivalent.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN EDUCATION
description: Current and/or special issues in education will vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: PRACTICUM IN ELEMENTARY MATHEMATICS
description: Directed observation and participation in elementary mathematics classrooms. Students will experience practical application of the elementary mathematics curriculum, including scope and sequence of skills, organization of instruction, teaching strategies, and evaluation and assessment techniques. Participation in an elementary classroom is required.
prerequisites: ED 308 (ECE majors) or ED 309 (Elementary Education majors) Students must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION
description: An overview of the history, theories, and issues of pre-primary educational programs with an emphasis on current research. Designed to help students with analysis/synthesis of information on critical issues concerning young children. Participation in an early childhood classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: MATERIALS AND METHODS IN EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION
description: Theoretical bases of early childhood curriculum development, design of curricula, organization of instruction, teaching strategies, and evaluation and assessment techniques. Participation in an early childhood classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT AND COMMUNICATION SKILLS
description: Study of knowledge, skills, and dispositions needed to plan for and facilitate developmentally appropriate communication experiences of young children. Participation in an early childhood classroom is required.
prerequisites: Students must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: NURSERY & PRESCHOOL EDUCATION
description: The study of appropriate educational settings and curricula for children birth to five. Participation in an early childhood classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: PREPARATION FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE IN THE SECONDARY SCHOOL
description: Supervised experience in planning, directing, and evaluating learning experiences in secondary education. One hour of seminar and three hours of lab. Grading is pass/no credit.
prerequisites: Passing PRAXIS Content and PLT Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

description: Supervised experience in planning, directing, and evaluating learning experiences in elementary and secondary education.
prerequisites: ED 411 or ED 421 Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: PRACTICUM
description: Supervised experience working with students in kindergarten, special education, or gifted education setting. Grading is pass/no credit.
prerequisites: Completion of or concurrent enrollment in ED 422 Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: CLINICAL PRACTICE-INTERNSHIP
description: Designed for Alternate Post-Baccalaureate Certification elementary and secondary education 271 candidates who have met requirements to receive a temporary teaching certificate and who are employed fulltime as classroom teachers in their academic majors. Candidates must complete six credits over two terms for completion of certification requirements. Pass/no credit.
prerequisites: Must be taken concurrently with ED 412 or ED 414 Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

title: PRINCIPLES OF TEACHING AND LEARNING
description: Principles of teaching and learning in secondary schools with an emphasis on alignment to standards, lesson plan writing, assessment, and teaching diverse learners. Participation in a secondary classroom is required.
prerequisites: Student must be admitted to TEPAR to take this course.

description: An introduction to digital arts and design. This course is designed to introduce the students to topics including design principles and the various software used in the field of digital art and design.

title: BASIC DESIGN

title: INTRODUCTION TO GAME ENGINES
description: An introduction to the pipelines and work-flows used in the current game industry, with a focus on the most popular game engines. An overview of the history of game engines will be introduced, along with some basic workflows to help students explore the capabilities of working in game engines. This course assumes you have no prior experience with 3D modeling software, animation, programing or work in game engines.

title: TECHNOLOGY PLANNING AND ADMINISTRATION
description: The management and development of skills in the following: technology planning and policies, resource and facility management, technology staff and personnel management, funding and budgetary issues, procedures and purchasing practices.

title: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT FOR K-12 TECHNOLOGY INTEGRATION
description: This course will focus on the development of specific technological skills and explore different methods of using technology in instruction, assessment, research, and professional development.

title: ADVANCED TELECOMMUNICATIONS AND DISTANCE EDUCATION
description: This course covers the foundation of telecommunications and distance learning. Topics to be studied include the history of distance education, the impact technology has on the traditional and electronic classroom, current trends, and research.

title: EDUCATIONAL TECHNOLOGY RESEARCH, EVALUATION, AND ASSESSMENT
description: This course will focus on researching the effectiveness of technology products and processes, selecting appropriate technology tools for assessment, and evaluating learner and program outcomes. This process includes the use of a needs assessment, program monitoring, stakeholder awareness and influences, and assessing effectiveness and efficiency.

title: SPECIAL PROBLEMS IN EDUCATION
description: Intensive study, research, and reporting on a significant problem in education. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY IN EDUATION
description: An independent study directed by an assigned member of the Education faculty. Formal paper required. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: CREATING A PROFESSIONAL LEARNING COMMUNITY
description: The Professional Learning Community (PLC) provides potential teacher leaders and administrators with a structure for continuing professional growth and information and activities aligned with best practices and current research on initiatives that support the Louisiana Accountability System, the School Improvement Process, and the Standards for School Principals in Louisiana.

title: LEADING WITH VISION
description: This course explores an area of school leadership that is essential for improving student achievement: developing a vision of teaching and learning that is shared by all stakeholders. It examines ways to develop, articulate, implement, and steward a shared vision.

title: LEGAL AND ETHICAL ISSUES IN EDUCATION
description: This course is designed to provide candidates with the fundamental concepts of American school law and how to apply the law to real world school settings. Included is coverage of specific Louisiana educational statues. Ethical theories and ideas will also be explored. The course will emphasize and focus on legal and ethical issues that teachers and administrators confront in their work.

title: UTILIZING DATA FOR SCHOOL IMPROVEMENT
description: This course focuses on the collection and analysis of data sources relevant in educational settings and emphasizes analyses of work samples, observations, inquiry data, artifacts, and standardized test scores. Throughout the course students collect and analyze school improvement data.

title: LEADING INSTRUCTION AND ASSESSMENT
description: A study of the role of organizational leadership in the development of instructional goals, instructional programs, evaluation procedures and procedures for educational change.

title: FUNDING PUBLIC EDUCATION
description: The study of problems and issues related to the appropriation and allocation of public financial resources for education.

title: FACILITATING HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT AND EMPOWERMENT
description: The study of concepts and practices in recruitment, selection, development, and utilization of professional human resources in educational organizations.

title: MOBILIZING THE COMMUNITY
description: The study of concepts and practices for developing and maintaining informed involvement of communities with public schools.

title: INTERNSHIP IN EDUCATIONAL LEADERSHIP
description: The internship, which will be required of all administrative program candidates, serves as the culminating experience and the capstone of the degree program. During the internship, candidates will assess the suitability of their skills and dispositions for administrative work, integrate skills and knowledge previously acquired, and become socialized into the administrative role under the supervision of a local school district administrator or other approved leader and a University faculty member. A minimum of 240 hours of field experience will be required for this course.

title: INTERNSHIP IN EDUCATIONAL LEADERSHIP
description: The internship, which will be required of all administrative program candidates, serves as the culminating experience and the capstone of the degree program. During the internship, candidates will assess the suitability of their skills and dispositions for administrative work, integrate skills and knowledge previously acquired, and become socialized into the administrative role under the supervision of a local school district administrator or other approved leader and a University faculty member. May be repeated for up to four credit hours.

title: INTRODUCTION TO GRADUATE RESEARCH
description: This course is a study of research methods and their empirical foundations. It provides basic practical knowledge on how to find, review, and critique academic and applied research in education and leadership. In addition, students will learn to develop appropriate research questions.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN EDUCATIONAL LEADERSHIP
description: Consent of the Department. Current and/or special issues on topics related to educational leadership will vary from term to term. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of lecture.
others: {'consent_of_the_department': 'Consent of the Department.', 'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture.'}

title: FOUNDATIONS IN STEM EDUCATION
description: The concept of STEM education will be explored including how this translates to actual classroom implementations of STEM as a metadiscipline. This course fosters the view of the STEM skill set as being integrated and interdependent, and as an essential element in real-world collaboration. Participants will also be introduced to materials and activities that emphasize STEM across the curriculum.

title: STEM CONTENT METHODS
description: The course will include application of new ideas through networking and collaboration designed to help educators structure opportunities for students to utilize a Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics skill set through investigation and application of science knowledge, applied mathematics, and utilization of tools of technology in order to design solutions to real-world problems. This course fosters the view of the STEM skill set as 275 being integrated and interdependent, and as an essential element in real-world collaboration. Educators will explore STEM resources, adapt existing activities to the STEM teaching philosophy, and create original STEM-based units.

title: TUTORING WRITING
description: A writing course that offers practical experience in tutoring other students in the Writing Center. Emphasizes the writing process and strategies for helping others improve their writing. May be repeated for up to three hours credit.
prerequisites: ENGL 105 and ENGL 115 with a GPA in those courses of 3.5 and consent of the Department
others: {'lecture': 'One hour of lecture', 'lab': 'three hours of lab'}

title: ADVANCED COMPOSITION
description: A generalized writing course for those wishing to improve their ability to communicate to a non-technical audience. Gives some attention to argumentation but focuses on exposition, description, and narration.

title: WRITING FICTION & POETRY I
description: An introductory creative writing course that offers workshop criticism of student work. For fiction, emphasizes techniques of point of view, dialogue, setting, and characterization; for poetry, techniques of open and closed forms, with special attention to contemporary methods.

title: INTRODUCTION TO LINGUISTICS
description: A study of syntax, semantics, phonology, and pragmatics. Emphasizes socio-linguistic topics such as dialectal variation, attitudes about language change, and differing conceptions of correctness and propriety.

title: DIGITAL RHETORIC
description: A survey of the theoretical foundations of digital rhetoric and multimedia theory, including topics such as circulation, audience, delivery, usability, and networked rhetoric. Students will incorporate concepts into practice with digital tools and produce multiple digital artifacts, such as videos, infographics, or memes.

title: SCIENCE FICTION AND FANTASY LITERATURE
description: The origin, development, and common themes of fantasy and science fiction as literary and cinematic subgenres, with attention to the distinguishing traits of these subgenres, their social and literary functions, and their variations from mainstream fiction.

title: LITERATURE OF THE OLD TESTAMENT
description: A study of selections from the Old Testament that represent such literary forms as the epic, lyric poetry, and tragedy. Emphasizes selections’ literary value.

title: LITERATURE OF THE NEW TESTAMENT
description: Interpretation of New Testament literature through rhetorical criticism and study of the influence of Classical Rhetoric on New Testament thought.

title: TECHNICAL WRITING
description: A specialized writing course for students in the sciences, computer science, engineering, and agriculture. Emphasizes proposals, reports, technical papers, and correspondence.

title: WRITING IN THE HUMANITIES
description: A specialized writing course for students in the humanities, including the arts and social sciences. Emphasizes analysis, explication, and evaluation.

title: GRANT WRITING FOR STEM PROGRAMS
description: Educators will research STEM funding prospects, interact with those funders, and plan, write, and submit grant proposals. The course will also guide educators on how to track and manage grant proposals and funded grants for fiscal and reporting purposes. This course fosters the view of the STEM skill set as being integrated and interdependent, and as an essential element in real-world collaboration.

description: This course will provide an interdisciplinary approach to integrating STEM into practice across the disciplines. Students will practice backwards design to develop their own STEM learning activity. Graduate students will implement their activity in a classroom and monitor student-learning outcomes.

title: STATICS
description: Vectorial treatment of resultants and equilibrium of force systems, centroids and centers of gravity, fluid statics, friction.

title: ENGLISH GRAMMAR REVIEW
description: A review of the fundamentals of grammar and punctuation. Emphasizes techniques for avoiding the most common problems in sentence structure and most common errors in mechanics. Does not satisfy general education requirement for freshman composition.

title: ENGLISH COMPOSITION I
description: A writing course that stresses exposition and argumentation and introduces students to library research. Employs selected readings to illustrate a variety of rhetorical strategies and to enhance critical reading skills.

title: PRACTICUM IN READING
description: A supervised practicum for students seeking reading specialist certification. Emphasis is on the application of specialized remedial techniques in a classroom setting.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab', 'field_experience': 'A minimum of 25 hours of field experience will be required'}

title: READING IN THE CONTENT AREAS
description: Strategies and practices for teaching reading in the content areas including Social Studies, Mathematics, English, Science and other areas. The focus is on middle school and high school classroom instruction.

title: HISTORY OF URBAN EDUCATION
description: The History or Urban Education exposes students to historical and contemporary urban education data and legislation. Specifically, this course investigates: (a) the interplay of race, power, and politics in urban schools; (b) underscores the concomitant impact these factors impose on urban children, their families, and the community-atlarge; and (c) highlights the resilience of these communities. As such, this course helps students cultivate a justiceoriented, culturally responsive mindset.

title: URBAN SCHOOLS AND THE COMMUNITY
description: Students explore research-based theories and best practices in school, family, and community collaboration. Educators will develop strategies for positively interacting with, relating to, and understanding the context of urban communities, their identified strengths and challenges, and citizens. The emphasis of the course is on students researching, creating, implementing, and evaluating an action plan for community partnerships in the urban setting

title: CULTURAL RESPONSIVE CLASSROOM ORGANIZATION AND MANAGEMENT
description: This course intends to critically address school discipline and urban schools. It offers specific approaches and strategies for enacting culturally responsive classroom management (CRCM). It explores how educators can establish an equitable and socially just classroom culture. Particular consideration is given to building mutually respectful relationships with diverse students, engaging parents/guardians/families from different cultural backgrounds, and creating and sustaining a safe, caring, and inclusive classroom using restorative practices that engender a positive learning environment.

title: URBAN SCHOOL REFORM
description: This course examines systemic school reform movements in the urban school context. Various reform strategies at different levels as well as theories, policies, practices, and controversies of various mechanisms of reform will be evaluated including the K-8 movement, small high schools, school choice (charters and vouchers), mayoral control, merit-pay, and alternative routes to teaching. Participants will synthesize information about school reform in urban schools and systems and will reflect on their role in this process. Final evaluation of reform strategies will be grounded in the effect these reforms are having on improving learning for all students in urban schools.

title: TEACHER AS CONSULTANT
description: Provides knowledge of educational consultation and skills necessary to be an effective consultant to regular and special education teachers concerning children who are experiencing learning and/or behavior problems at school.

title: METHODS OF TEACHING THE GIFTED
description: A comprehensive review of teaching-learning strategies that can be used in the implementation of a curriculum for gifted students.

title: EDUCATIONAL TECHNOLOGY APPLIED TO THE CLASSROOM
description: A hands on, project-centered course using technology in the classroom, with attention to selection of hardware and software, use of multimedia authoring tools and communications technology in the development of instructional materials.

title: TECHNOLOGY LEADERSHIP IN SCHOOLS
description: Development of foundation skills for managing technology at the school site. Skills include school-wide planning that incorporates instructional design, curriculum integration with standards, and logistics of technology implementation, training, and evaluation.

title: INTRODUCTION TO SCREENWRITING
description: An introduction to narrative theory, Hollywood’s approach to storytelling, and the conventions of writing for the screen.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS
description: Various topics selected from the areas of literature, writing, linguistics, film, or pedagogy and intended for the non-specialist. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of 6 term hours.
prerequisites: Completion of sophomore English requirement or consent of the Department

title: HISTORY OF THE ENGLISH LANGUAGE
description: A study of the development of the language from Old English to Modern English. Emphasizes changes in grammar, phonology, and vocabulary.

title: EARLY CLASSICS IN TRANSLATION
description: A survey of epic, lyric, and dramatic literature, in translation, from the Greek and Roman period to late medieval times. Emphasizes such writers as Homer, Sappho, Sophocles, Ovid, Horace, Virgil, and Dante.

title: CHAUCER: THE CANTERBURY TALES
description: A close study of The Canterbury Tales with some attention to Chaucer’s other works and his language. Places Chaucer in historical context; considers his use of such medieval genres as the fabliau, the beast fable, and the romance; and explores the issue of the collection’s artistic unity.

title: STUDY IN MEDIEVAL LITERATURE
description: A study of a related body of works from the middle ages. Emphasizes a genre such as drama or a theme such as Arthurian legend.

title: SHAKESPEARE: EARLY WORKS
description: A study of Shakespeare’s developing artistry to about 1600. Emphasizes the drama, with some attention to the poetry and the intellectual and cultural milieu.

title: SHAKESPEARE: LATER WORKS
description: A study of Shakespeare’s maturing artistry after about 1600. Emphasizes achievements in drama, with some attention to the poetry and the intellectual and cultural milieu.

title: STUDIES IN EARLY-MODERN LITERATURE
description: A study of a related body of works from about 1485 to 1660. Topics may vary each term. Emphasizes a genre such as drama or a theme such as humanism.

title: SHAKESPEARE AND FILM
description: A study of interrelationships between contemporary cinema and the timeless drama of William Shakespeare. Primary focus will be on film as an interpretive prism, with some attention paid to the history of Shakespeare on film. Emphasizes the comparative study of scenes from selected plays.

title: ENGLISH COMPOSITION PRACTICUM
description: A supplementary review of instruction in exposition, argumentation, and library research in ENGL 105 . This review builds upon the assignments in ENGL 105 to emphasize and enhance writing strategies and critical reading skills.
prerequisites: Department permission and concurrent enrollment in ENGL 105
others: {'laboratory_instruction': 'Two hours laboratory instruction', 'grading': 'Grading is on a pass/no credit basis'}

title: ENGLISH COMPOSITION II
description: A writing course that further develops the writing, research, and critical thinking skills acquired in ENGL 105 . Students will engage with a variety of writing genres and rhetorical modes, including analysis, description, and evaluation; students will also learn how to write collaboratively. Course content and writing projects will be oriented around a theme of the instructor’s choosing.

title: HONORS ENGLISH COMPOSITION II
description: Offers reading and writing assignments enriched for accelerated learning.
prerequisites: ENGL 105 and consent of the Department.
others: {'lecture': 'Three hours of lecture', 'section_info': 'A section of ENGL 115 open to students who have tested out of or performed exceptionally well in ENGL 105'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO LITERARY STUDIES
description: This course introduces students to the formal study of literature, and to trends in criticism and theory. Students learn techniques of close contextual reading, interpretive strategies, techniques of literary analysis, and strategies for writing effective analytical papers. The course also provides a hands-on guide to literary research, including Electronic databases and the Internet.

title: INTRODUCTION TO RHETORIC AND WRITING STUDIES
description: This foundational course for the Writing in Multiple Media concentration introduces students to the formal study and practice of rhetoric, as well as covering trends in the field and major theories. Students learn fundamental tools for rhetorical practice and criticism, such as the rhetorical canons, genre theory, stasis theory, and more. The course also provides students with individualized exploration of career paths in rhetoric.

title: SURVEY OF BRITISH LITERATURE I
description: A survey of English literature from the Anglo-Saxon era through the eighteenth century. Emphasizes such writers as Chaucer, Spenser, Shakespeare, Donne, Milton, Dryden, Swift, and Pope.

title: SURVEY OF BRITISH LITERATURE II
description: A survey of the literature from the end of the eighteenth century to the present. Emphasizes such writers as Wordsworth, Keats, Tennyson, Browning, Yeats, Joyce, and Woolf.

title: INTRODUCTION TO AMERICAN LITERATURE
description: The study of major American authors from the colonial through the modern periods. Emphasizes such writers as Edwards, Franklin, Whitman, Hawthorne, Dickinson, Twain, Eliot, and Faulkner.

title: INTRODUCTION TO FICTION
description: An introduction to the short story, the novella, and the novel. Emphasizes works by American and British writers, with some attention to works in translation from other cultures.
others: {'lecture': 'Three hours of lecture', 'louisiana_common_course': '[ CENL 2303/Introduction to Fiction'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO POETRY AND DRAMA
description: An introduction to the lyric, the ballad, and other poetic forms and to tragedy, comedy, and other dramatic forms. Emphasizes works by American and British writers, with some attention to works in translation from other cultures.
others: {'lecture': 'Three hours of lecture', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CENL 2313/Introduction to Poetry and/or Drama'}

title: PROPOGANDA AND MASS COMMUNICATION
description: The study and application of major theories in the field of persuasion. Students will examine and synthesize comparative theories in the Field ranging from Aristotle through contemporary writers. Students will analyze emerging issues from case studies and media applications using appropriate field methodology.

title: ORGANIZATIONAL COMMUNICATION
description: The study and application of major theories in the field of organizational communication, including vertical and horizontal internal interaction, as well as external and gatekeeping functions. The course demands particular attention to postmodern theories and critical responses in applied communication.

title: GROUP DYNAMICS
description: The study and application of major theories in the field of group dynamics. Students will examine and synthesize comparative theories focused on the communication interactions of a single group including verbal and nonverbal communication, emergent leadership roles, and power shifts. Students will analyze emerging issues with technology and social networking group affiliation.

title: INTERCULTURAL COMMUNICATION
description: The study and application of major theories in the field of intercultural communication. Students will examine and synthesize comparative theories from the field focused on the communication interactions of a single culture including verbal and nonverbal interactions, semiotic interpretation. Students will analyze emerging issues with technology and social networking across cultures.

title: INTRODUCTION TO COUNSELING
description: This course is designed to provide the student with an overview of counseling as a profession. It will introduce the student to the scientist/practitioner model and define the subject matter of counseling, the target population the counselor seeks to serve, the technical tools needed for practice in the years ahead, the current unresolved issues and controversies in the field, and how these will affect the theories and techniques in the future.

title: THEORY AND PRACTICE OF COUNSELING
description: Study of the major counseling theories including the psychodynamic, humanistic, and cognitive-behavioral approaches, and an introduction to systems theory.

title: ANALYSIS OF THE INDIVIDUAL
description: Techniques and materials available for study of the individual; interpretation and synthesis of individual inventory data and their application in the counseling process.
prerequisites: Graduate standing or consent of the Department

title: GROUP PROCESS
description: Introduction to group process in general, as well as to the formation of a broad variety of the therapeutic and counseling groups. Topics include a review of theory-based techniques from a variety of orientations.

title: COUNSELING METHODS AND TECHNIQUES
description: In-depth study of the major counseling skills, techniques, and strategies to include assessment, interviewing, rapport building, counseling, and termination skills, combined with appropriate laboratory/field experiences designed to illustrate the characteristics and behaviors that influence the counseling process.
prerequisites: COUN 720 or consent of the Department

title: MARRIAGE AND FAMILY COUNSELING
description: An overview of family systems theory, practice, and research. Contemporary professional issues in family therapy will also be considered.

title: SEVENTEENTH-CENTURY BRITISH LITERATURE
description: A study of literature produced between 1600 and 1660, focusing on literary responses to contemporary social, political, and religious issues. Includes such authors as Donne, Jonson, Bacon, Herbert, and Marvell.

title: MILTON
description: A close study of Milton’s Paradise Lost in the context of his earlier poetry and prose. Examines Milton’s responses to his cultural milieu and explores his revision of literary forms, including the sonnet, elegy, masque, drama, and epic.

title: NARRATIVE STRUCTURES IN MULTIPLE MEDIA
description: An exploration of various narrative structures from Classical epic to computer games and graphic novels.

title: VISUAL RHETORIC
description: An introduction to the analysis and production of persuasive visuals and visual-verbal texts. Includes principles of graphic design, professional document design, and image production as well as how to use these principles for persuasive purposes.

title: WRITING FICTION & POETRY II
description: An advanced creative writing course that offers workshop criticism of student work. For fiction, emphasizes techniques of point of view, dialogue, setting, and characterization; for poetry, techniques of open and closed forms, with special attention to contemporary methods.
prerequisites: ENGL 228 or consent of the Department

title: RESORATION AND EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY BRITISH LITERATURE
description: A study of the literature from the restoration of the monarchy in 1660 to about 1798, with attention to the emergence of neoclassicism and other aspects of the intellectual and artistic milieu. Emphasizes such writers as Dryden, Wycherly, Pope, Swift, Johnson, and Goldsmith.

title: BRITISH NOVEL TO 1900
description: A study of the development of the British novel from its beginnings through the nineteenth century. Emphasizes such writers as Fielding, Sterne, Austen, Dickens, the Brontes, Eliot, and Hardy.

title: 20TH CENTURY LITERATURE
description: A study of British and/or U.S. literature from 1900-2000. Emphasizes British literature, U.S.literature, or transatlanticism.

title: BRITISH ROMANTIC PERIOD
description: A study of British literature from about 1780 to the coronation of Queen Victoria in 1837. Emphasizes such writers as Blake, Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, Shelley, Keats, Hazlitt, and de Quincey.

title: MODERN POETRY
description: A study of developments in British and American poetry from about 1870 to the present. Emphasizes such poets as Hopkins, Eliot, Yeats, Pound, Frost, Stevens, Roethke, and Rich.

title: BRITISH VICTORIAN PERIOD
description: A study of British literature from the coronation of Queen Victoria in 1837 to her death in 1901. Emphasizes such writers as the Brownings, Tennyson, Arnold, the Rossettis, Carlyle, Ruskin, and Pater.

title: ROMANTIC MOVEMENT IN AMERICAN LITERATURE
description: A survey of American literature from about 1820 to the Civil War. Emphasizes such authors as Irving, Emerson, Thoreau, Hawthorne, Melville, Douglass, Whitman, and Dickinson.

title: MODERN DRAMA
description: A study of developments in British and American drama from about 1870 to the present, with some attention to European influences and contemporaries. Emphasizes such writers as Ibsen, Shaw, Beckett, Pinter, O’Neill, Williams, Brecht, and Albee.

title: AGE OF REALISH IN AMERICAN LITERATURE
description: A survey of American literature from the Civil War to World War I. Emphasizes such authors as Twain, Howells, James, Chopin, Crane, Norris, Dreiser, and Wharton.

title: 19TH CENTURY U.S. LITERATURE
description: A study of U.S. literature from 1800 to 1900. Emphasizes such authors as Washington Irving, Frederick Douglass, Rebecca Harding Davis, Mark Twain, Louisa May Alcott, Emily Dickinson, Stephen Crane, and Charles W. Chesnutt.

title: 21ST-CENTURY LITERATURE
description: A study of British and/or U.S. literature from 2000 to the present day. Emphasizes such authors as Zadie Smith, Pat Barker, Viet Thanh Nguyen, Margaret Atwood, Alison Bechdel, Roy Scranton, Cormac McCarthy, Kazuo Ishiguro, Ben Fountain, Colson Whitehead, and Stephen King.

title: AMERICAN NOVEL TO 1900
description: A study of developments in the novel from the beginnings to 1900. Emphasizes such writers as Cooper, Hawthorne, Melville, Twain, James, Howells, and Crane.

title: LITERATURE OF SOUTH
description: A study of developments in the literature written by Southerners, with some attention to the historical and cultural backgrounds. Emphasizes such writers as Cable, Chopin, Faulkner, O’Connor, Welty, Williams, and Warren.

title: AFRICAN AMERICAN LITERATURE
description: A study of developments in African American literature, with an emphasis on historical and cultural backgrounds. Emphasizes such writers as Wheatley, Harper, Walker, Douglass, Washington, Dunbar, Chesnutt, Du Bois, Hughes, McKay, Toomer, Larsen, Hurston, Wright, Ellison, Brooks, Angelou, Baldwin, Giovanni, and Morrison.

title: HISTORY & DEVELOPMENT OF LITERARY CRITICISM
description: A survey of the history and development of theories of literary criticism from the classical period to the present. Emphasizes historical and cultural influences on theoretical approaches, with some attention to contemporary applications.

title: THEORY AND PRACTICE OF COMPOSITION
description: A broad survey of the evolution of composition theory from the field’s inception to the present. The course will explore varying methods, techniques, and notions of composition theory and pedagogy. Students will also examine scholarly movements in composition theory such as the process movement, the cognitive view of writing, the social “turn” in composition theory, and others. Three hours of seminar.

title: WORLD LITERATURE
description: A survey of literature representing Latin American, European, African, Asian, and Middle Eastern cultures. Covers mythology, folklore, and such ancient works as the epic Gilgamesh, as well as works by more modern writers such as Flaubert, Chekov, Ibsen, Marquez, Achebe, and Kawabata. Three hours of lecture.

title: SENIOR CAPSTONE
description: A course designed to encourage majors to synthesize their course of study in order to enlarge their appreciation of the aesthetic, historical, and cultural values of literature. Includes attention to professional issues, graduate school requirements, and career opportunities. Three hours of lecture.

title: SEMINAR IN ENGLISH
description: A variable-topics course for advanced students interested in studying a topic more deeply than regular offerings permit. May focus on a major author, a literary period, a genre, literary criticism, creative writing, linguistics, or pedagogy. May be repeated once for credit. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Completion of sophomore English requirement and consent of the Department

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES IN ENGLISH
description: A special-topics course that permits students who have demonstrated exceptional competence to pursue a special interest under the direction of a faculty member. Involves extensive readings, original research, and a series of reports or critical analyses. May be repeated once for credit. Three hours of research.
prerequisites: Completion of six hours of English at the 300-level or above and consent of the Department

title: WRITING INTERNSHIP
description: A writing course offering professional writing experience for English majors or students in the Writing Specialization. May involve oncampus appointment or writing and editing for a local business or governmental agency. Ten to fifteen hours of lab per week. Grading is on a pass/no credit basis. May be repeated for a maximum of six credit hours.
prerequisites: Senior standing, at least a 3.0 GPA, and consent of the Department

title: HISTORY OF THE ENGLISH LANGUAGE
description: A study of the development of the language from Old English to Modern English. Emphasizes changes in grammar, phonology, and vocabulary. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: EARLY CLASSICS IN TRANSLATION
description: A survey of epic, lyric, and dramatic literature, in translation, from the Greek and Roman period to late medieval times. Emphasizes such writers as Homer, Sappho, Sophocles, Ovid, Horace, Virgil, and Dante. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: CHAUCER: THE CANTERBURY TALES
description: A close study of The Canterbury Tales with some attention to Chaucer’s other works and his language. Places Chaucer in historical context; considers his use of such medieval genres as the fabliau, the beast fable, and the romance; and explores the issue of the collection’s artistic unity. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: STUDIES IN MEDIEVAL LITERATURE
description: A study of a related body of works from the middle ages. Emphasizes a genre such as drama or a theme such as Arthurian legend. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN
description: This project-based course encourages the student to go beyond the conventions of traditional graphic design.
others: {'recommended': 'DA 318', 'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: ADOBE PHOTOSHOP II
description: Continuation of DA 315 with an emphasis on further enhancing the student’s use of Photoshop’s artistic tools to create digital art and photo-manipulation.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: PACKAGE DESIGN
description: Introduction to the concepts associated with consumer packaging design with an emphasis on placing two-dimensional art on three-dimensional objects.
prerequisites: DA 315 and DA 318 , or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: SHAKESPEARE: EARLY WORKS
description: A study of Shakespeare’s developing artistry to about 1600. Emphasizes the drama, with some attention to the poetry and the intellectual and cultural milieu. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations.

title: DIGITAL ILLUSTRATION II
description: Continuation of DA 318 with an emphasis on further enhancing the use and illustration software as an artistic tool used in the creation of digital art.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: SHAKESPEARE: LATER WORKS
description: A study of Shakespeare’s maturing artistry after about 1600. Emphasizes achievements in drama, with some attention to the poetry and the intellectual and cultural milieu. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations.

title: DIGITAL VIDEO EDITING II
description: Continuation of DA 319 with an emphasis on creating a complete short film. Will include an introduction to storyboarding, compositing, and sound editing.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: RENAISSANCE POETRY AND DRAMA
description: A survey of the poetry from about 1500 to about 1600 and of the non-Shakespearean drama from about 1500 to the closing of the theaters in 1642. Emphasizes such authors as Wyatt, Sydney, Spenser, Kyd, Marlowe, Jonson, and Webster. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations.

title: AFTER EFFECTS II
description: Continuation of DA 320 with an emphasis on further developing the use of After Effects as a creative tool.
others: {'recommended': 'DA 319', 'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: SHAKESPEARE AND FILM
description: This seminar explores the interrelationships between contemporary cinema and the timeless drama of William Shakespeare. Primary focus will be on film as an interpretive prism, with some attention paid to the history of Shakespeare on film, the playwright’s script as “source,” and ways in which dramatists and directors seek to control audience response. Emphasizes the comparative study of scenes from selected plays. Students will discover, through discussion, writing exercises and oral reports the significant differences and surprising similarities between Shakespeare’s stage productions and the productions of those authors who translate his works to cinema.

title: INTERACTIVE DESIGN II
description: Topics designed to further enhance student understanding and development of user-interface design to include advanced user-interface construction, user experience, and prototyping.

description: Topics designed to further enhance student understanding of the development and management of websites to include advanced web graphics, aesthetics, and usability as well as mobile and responsive design.
