description: Intermediate group instruction in piano. A continuation of MUS 150 , with a more advanced treatment of chords, inversions, techniques, and repertoire. Work in piano lab with software programs and keyboard is necessary.
prerequisites: MUS 150 or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'three hours of lab'}

title: MUSIC EDUCATION FOR ELEMENTARY TEACHERS
description: Fundamentals of music applied to the elementary school classroom.

title: MUSIC HISTORY I
description: A survey of the development of musical style in the Renaissance, Baroque, and Classic periods.

title: MUSIC HISTORY II
description: A survey of the development of musical style in the Romantic period and twentieth century, and a survey of American music.

title: NONPROFIT BOARDS AND LEADERSHIP
description: This course explores the history, structure, and function of nonprofit governance and boards of directors. It demonstrates the role of boards as agents of and for social change and social justice at the organizational and societal levels. It provides comparisons of various sizes and types of nonprofit and philanthropic organizations.

title: DIRECTED PROJECT IN FAITH-BASED ORGANIZATIONS
description: This course offers students a hands-on opportunity to work with a faith-based organization (FBO) on a strategic project using the Leap Ambassadors Community’s Performance Practice framework. Students explore how faith traditions shape governance and operations while developing inclusive, neutral approaches. The course concludes with a ready-to-implement project plan.

title: NONPROFIT FINANCIAL OVERSIGHT
description: Explores the application of financial management within nonprofit and philanthropic organizations, including but not limited to financial planning and budgeting, cash flow, short- and long-term financing, and endowment management practices and policies. Outlines the ethical considerations of nonprofit financial management.
prerequisites: Certificate student or consent from the Department

title: FUND DEVELOPMENT AND GRANTS
description: The student will explore the tools to help their organizations - schools, nonprofit agencies, community service groups, and businesses - successfully compete for grant funds and other forms of fund development. Methods for accomplishing these goals will primarily involve problem-based/project-based learning.
prerequisites: Certificate student or consent from the Department

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Special topics are selected from areas of significance in nonprofit administration. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six hours.

title: DEVELOPMENT AND THEORIES OF PHILANTHROPY AND NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: A foundational course examining the origins and societal role of private nonprofit organizations, including their social, political, economic, cultural, and ideological importance in American society, along with key theories of nonprofits and philanthropy.

title: NONPROFIT LAW AND ETHICS
description: A survey of legal concepts which affect the administration of human services from constitutional, statutory and common or case law. Particular emphasis will be given to such topics as organization, authority and procedures of administrative agencies, judicial review of administrative decisions; tort liability; civil rights and legislation; client rights and privacy, contracts, employee relations and due process.

title: PRINCIPLES OF GRANT WRITING
description: An exploration of researching and identifying grant opportunities most applicable to nonprofit organizations and understanding the process from beginning to end, including reporting to grant funders.

title: NONPROFIT RESEARCH AND PROGRAM EVALUATION
description: This course explores various theories and methods of research and program evaluation for nonprofit organizations. Students wil learn the fundamentals of qualitative and quantitative research methods relevant to the third sector. Using case studies on topics like outcome measurement and social return on investment, the course equips students with skills to evaluate program effectiveness and determines if organizations are achieving meaningful social outcomes.

title: NONPROFIT HUMAN RESOURCE ADMINISTRATION
description: Current issues in human resource administration for employees of nonprofit organizations. Topics include such areas as recruitment, staff development, performance, evaluation, labor-management issues, and affirmative action.

title: DIVERSITY IN NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Examines the characteristics of diverse populations across the lifespan to effectively develop inclusive community programs and services.

title: VOLUNTEER AND STAFF MANAGEMENT
description: Explores the role, value, and dynamics of employees and volunteers in fulfilling the missions of nonprofit and philanthropic organizations. Studies organizational theory and behavior as they apply in nonprofit and voluntary organizations. Introduces human resource management techniques and strategies for advancing group and team dynamics and their implication for nonprofit organizational performance and mission attainment.

title: NONPROFIT MARKETING, COMMUNICATIONS, AND PUBLIC RELATIONS
description: Study of principles and practices for marketing nonprofit organizations, including the dynamics of marketing the mission in a nonprofit context. Students will also learn current strategies and techniques for successful communications, public relations, and technology within nonprofit and philanthropic organizations. The class project will be developing a nonprofit marketing plan that includes social media marketing strategies.

title: RESEARCH METHODS IN THE SOCIAL SCIENCES
description: This course presents an overview of the basic methods and techniques used in social sciences research. It includes descriptive and inferential statistics, hypothesis testing, population and same identification, data collection, statistical analysis, and research report presentation with special emphasis on the areas of criminal justice, political science, and sociology.
cross_listed: Also listed as SOCL 330 , CJ 330 , and POLI 330 .

title: PRACTICUM IN NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: This course explores strategic planning, marketing, legal issues, program assessment, fundraising, development, financial planning, risk management, social media, human resources management, and organizational communication within nonprofit organizations and the particular challenges involved differing from for-profit organizations. Involves applying the concepts to a service-learning consulting project for a nonprofit organization.
cross_listed: Also listed as POLI 392 and SOCL 392 .

title: NONPROFIT AND VOLUNTARY SECTOR
description: This course provides an overview of the nonprofit and voluntary sector as well as nonprofit administrative practices. Students will explore different aspects of nonprofit administration and the size and scope of the sector as a whole.
prerequisites: One of the following - 9 hours of NPA, certificate student, or Department consent

title: ADMINISTRATIVE LAW OF NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Explores the various law practices of nonprofit organizations. Topics include but are not limited to nonprofit tax law, incorporation, conflicts of interest, fiscal sponsorship, and accountability practices for nonprofits.
prerequisites: One of the following: (9 hours of NPA, certificate student, or department consent)

title: SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS
description: Analysis of major social institutions such as education, family, politics, and religion.
prerequisites: NPA 192 , SOCL 105 , SOCL 107 , or consent of the Department

title: UNDERSTANDING AND MANAGING FAITH-BASED ORGANIZATIONS
description: This course explores academic and theoretical perspectives on faith-based organizations (FBOs) in the nonprofit sector, focusing on their unique characteristics, challenges, and contributions to society. Students will delve into the rationale, development, unique roles, organizational structures, and collaboration strategies of these entities. Through a combination of readings, case studies, videos, and writing projects, students will gain understanding necessary to lead and support FBOs. By the end of the course, participants will be equipped to make meaningful contributions to their communities and the missions of faith-based initiatives.

title: NONPROFIT BOARDS AND LEADERSHIP
description: This course explores the history, structure, and function of nonprofit governance and boards of directors. It demonstrates the role of boards as agents of and for social change and social justice at the organizational and societal levels. It provides comparisons of various sizes and types of nonprofit and philanthropic organizations.
prerequisites: One of the following - 9 hours of NPA, certificate student, or Department consent

title: NONPROFIT GOVERNANCE AND DECISION-MAKING
description: Analysis of the respective roles of board, executive director, staff and volunteers in nonprofit organizations. Techniques for achieving effective board meetings and committee work, the development of board members, and policy development.

title: NONPROFIT MARKETING AND STRATEGIC COMMUNICATIONS
description: This course explores the principles and practices of marketing and strategic communication within the nonprofit sector. Key topics include branding, audience engagement, message development, media relations, and the use of digital and traditional marketing tools to achieve organizational goals. Students will analyze case studies and create comprehensive marketing and communication plans tailored to the unique challenges and opportunities faced by nonprofit organizations.

title: NONPROFIT FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
description: The conceptual and technical skills needed to manage financial and strategic control problems facing nonprofit administrators. Topics to be addressed include financial planning and strategy, financial reports, types and sources of funding, cash managements, banking relations, liabilities, investment policies and procedures, safeguarding assets, and financial policies.

title: NONPROFIT RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT
description: An exploration of the theory, research, and best practice techniques on resource development for nonprofit organizations. Topics to be addressed include management of fund development, ethics and governance issues, environment and institutional readiness, annual giving programs, major gifts, audiences and environments, and support ingredients for successful fundraising.

title: SEMINAR IN PLANNED GIVING
description: An exploration of aspects of planned giving. Major topics explored are types of planned giving, deferred gifts, a profile of planned givers, the motivations for planned giving, planners and their roles, the giver and charitable institution connection, estate planning and planned giving, acknowledgments and recognition, the role of charitable boards, the role of chief executives, and the role of the planned giving professional.

title: SEMINAR IN DEVELOPMENT
description: An exploration of selected topics on fundraising which draws upon the research and theory of physiology, organizational behavior, and management. Topics to be explored include why people give, organizational strategies to maximize giving, shaping roles for giving within an organization, strategic organizations of various size, and future directions.

title: STRUCTURE AND PROCESS IN NONPROFIT ADMINISTRATION
description: The capstone of the nonprofit administration program, this course examines organizational behavior, structure, processes, and change as influenced by the practice of management. Implications of organizational design are explored. Applications are made to nonprofit organizations.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY
description: The student is expected to do original research through readings or data collection with the outcome being a manuscript of project worthy of publication in a professional journal or presentation at a professional conference. The manuscript or project should evidence creativity, synthesis of positions and previously established thoughts, and implications for policy, application, or future theoretical development. This course may be repeated once for credit.

title: NONPROFIT DIRECTED PROJECT
description: This course provides a practical project platform to train students to properly conduct and understand research in the context of nonprofit leadership.

title: EXPLORING PHILOSOPHY
description: An introduction to critically thinking about the major issues and ideas found throughout the history of philosophy.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES IN MARKETING
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the guidance of a member of the marketing faculty. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of research.

title: MATHEMATICAL CONCEPTS FOR K-8 TEACHERS
description: This course is designed to blend pedagogical instruction and mathematical concepts for the effective teaching of mathematics in grades K-8. Credit will apply only to programs in Education. Three hours of lecture.

title: MATHEMATICAL CONCEPTS FOR ELEMENTARY TEACHERS
description: This course is designed to blend instructional techniques and mathematical concepts related to the teaching of grades K-5. Credit will apply only to programs in Education. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MTED 700 or consent of the Department

title: MATHEMATICAL CONCEPTS FOR MIDDLE GRADE TEACHERS
description: This course is designed to blend instructional techniques and mathematical concepts related to the teaching of grades 6-8. Credit for this course will apply only to the Master of Education degree. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MTED 700 or consent of the Department

title: INTUITIVE GEOMETRY FOR K-8 TEACHERS
description: Credit will not be given for both this course and MTED 706 . This course is designed to provide the geometric concepts necessary for the teaching of geometry in grades K-8. Topics to be covered include patterns of polygons in the plane, polyhedra and tessellations of space, motions in the physical world, translations, similarities, congruence, and isometries. Credit will apply only to programs in Education. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MATH 217 or consent of the Department

title: INTUITIVE GEOMETRY FOR 6-12 TEACHERS
description: Credit will not be given for both this course and MTED 705 . This course is designed to provide the concepts in geometry necessary for the effective teaching of geometry in grades 6-12. Topics to be covered include motion geometry (translations, rotations, reflections, magnification), similarity, congruence, elementary concepts of topology, number patterns in geometry and constructions. Credit for this course will apply only to the Master of Education degree. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MATH 407 or consent of the Department

title: MATHEMATICAL MODELING FOR TEACHERS
description: Problem formulation, as well as problem-solving activities, suitable for use in courses in grades K-12 is covered. Emphasis will be placed on concepts from the social, physical and biological sciences. Credit will apply only to programs in Education. Three hours of lecture.

title: ELEMENTARY STATISTICS FOR TEACHERS
description: This course will cover descriptive statistics, simulations, normal distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, correlation and regression. Concepts, problems, and examples from business, social sciences and the natural sciences that are appropriate for use in precollege curriculum are included. Credit for this course will apply only to the Master of Education degree. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MATH 141 or consent of the Department

title: MATHEMATICS FOR TEACHERS
description: Topics to be announced by the Departments of Mathematics and Education. Credit will apply only to programs in Education. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of lecture.

title: UNIVERSITY CHORALE
description: Intensive study of choral music of all periods, including preparation for public performance. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six hours. Three hours of lab.

title: LOGICAL REASONING
description: An introduction to the basic principles of deductive and inductive reasoning, emphasizing the art of reasoning and its practical application.

title: ETHICS
description: An analysis and evaluation of the major ethical issues of contemporary society in the light of the traditional ethical theories of western culture.

title: HISTORY OF PHILOSOPHY I: ANCIENT AND MEDIVEVAL
description: An exploration of the philosophies of the major philosophers of the ancient and medieval world.

title: HISTORY OF PHILOSOPHY II: RENAISSANCE THROUGH THE 19TH CENTURY
description: A presentation and analysis of the philosophies of the major philosophers from the Renaissance through the nineteenth century.

title: EXISTENTIALISM
description: A presentation and analysis of the major existential philosophers.

title: SEMINAR IN PHILOSOPHY
description: Selected topics from current issues and problems in philosophy. Will vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit a maximum of 6 hours.

title: EXISTENTIALISM
description: A presentation and analysis of the major existential philosophers. The course will present these philosophers through a variety of hermeneutical lenses. Students will be expected write and present a critical research paper on some aspect of existentialism.

title: SEMINAR IN PHILOSOPHY
description: Selected topics from current issues and problems in philosophy. Students will interact with and respond to these philosophical issues through research papers and class presentations. Will vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit a maximum of 6 hours.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN PHILOSOPHY
description: Special topics in the areas of metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, aesthetics, philosophical anthropology, philosophy of religion and political philosophy. Topics will vary from term to term. May be repeated for six hours credit.

title: PHYSICAL SCIENCE
description: A survey course in physics with attention given to recent discoveries and applications of physical science knowledge. This course is not intended for the student who wishes to pursue further work in any of the areas of physical science and may not be substituted for the basic course in any of these fields.
prerequisites: MATH 007 or sufficiently high score on a math diagnostic test

title: PHYSICAL SCIENCE
description: A survey of selected chemistry topics with attention given to recent discoveries and applications including the environmental impact of chemical technology. This course is not intended for the student who wishes to pursue further work in any of the areas of physical science and may not be substituted for the basic course in any of these fields. Three hours of lecture.

title: APPLIED CHEMISTRY LABORATORY
description: A course designed to enhance knowledge of chemistry laboratory techniques, especially those which illustrate the importance of chemistry in everyday life. Experiments will consider water purification, preparation of detergents and perfumes, analysis of milk, vitamin and mineral content of foods, radioactivity, acid rain, and the study of aspirin, caffeine, and fermentation. Will not substitute for CHEM 121L . One hour of instruction and two hours of lab.
prerequisites: Registration or credit in CHEM1 10, CHEM 106 , or PHSC 106

title: SELECTED TOPICS IN PHYSICAL SCIENCE
description: Topics will be selected from areas such as astronomy, physics, astrophysics, geophysics, and cosmology. The number of credit hours (one, two, or three) depends upon the topic and the breadth and depth of the coverage. One to three hours of lecture.

title: SCIENCE LABORATORY DEMONSTRATION FOR ELEMENTARY TEACHERS
description: Methods of using the science laboratory demonstration in teaching elementary school science. For students in the College of Education and in-service elementary science teachers. Three hours of lecture and demonstration.
prerequisites: Nine term-hours of biological and physical science including PHSC 106

title: PROJECT-BASED SCIENCE EDUCATION
description: Project-based Science Education for grades 7-12. This course is for both Science and Education majors. One hour of lecture and two hours of off-campus instruction and mentorship to students in local area schools and/or community learning centers.
prerequisites: Nine hours of biological or physical science, including PHSC 106 or CHEM 121

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN SCIENCE AND MODERN MAN
description: A humanistic approach to special topics to be selected from chemistry, physics, and cosmology. May be repeated for a maximum of twelve term-hours. Topics vary from term to term. Three hours of seminar.

title: INTRO ELEC CIRCUITS
description: This course is an introduction to electrical circuits, both D.C. and A.C., and will serve as prerequisite for PHYS 220 . Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MATH 122 or consent of the Department

title: PROJECTS IN MICROELECTRONICS AND AUTOMATION
description: Introductory project-based learning in a hands-on electronics lab with mentorship from both faculty and experienced teaching assistants. The projects offer real-world examples of key principles in electronics and automation. Students receive an introduction to circuit design and the programming of automated devices.

title: COMPREHENSIVE ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING
description: For Physics and Petroleum Engineering majors. Elementary circuits, devices, and systems in electrical engineering. Three hours of lecture.

title: ELECTRONICS
description: A basic or introductory course stressing the properties and applications of diodes, transistors, integrated circuits, and other circuit devices. The emphasis in the laboratory is placed on the design, fabrication, and testing of circuits utilizing the devices mentioned above. The lecture cannot be taken without the laboratory. Three hours of lecture and three hours of lab.
prerequisites: PHYS 120 or consent of the Department

title: PHYSICS FOR MIDDLE SCHOOL TEACHERS
description: A course intended primarily for current or future middle school science teachers. Course covers properties of matter, forces and motion, fluids, simple machines, heat, sound, electricity and magnetism.
prerequisites: MATH 121 or consent of the Department

title: GENERAL PHYSICS I
description: For the premedical student and the nonphysical science student. Study of mechanics, heat and sound. Three hours of lecture and demonstration.

title: MEDICAL PHYSICS
description: A course primarily for students interested in the health sciences. Covers the physics of the human body and of the equipment used for medical diagnosis and treatment.
prerequisites: PHYS 252 or consent of the Department

title: GENERAL PHYSICS LABORATORY I
description: Two-hour laboratory course to accompany PHYS 251.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MICROPROCESSORS
description: A hands-on learning experience with microprocessors, specifically the Intel 8085, for control and applications in such areas as data acquisition storage and retrieval and process control. The emphasis is on learning to use a microprocessor for control and on interfacing it to devices, systems, instruments and machines.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'three hours of lab'}

title: GENERAL PHYSICS II
description: For the premedical student and the in physical science student. Study of light, electricity and magnetism, and topics in modern physics. Three hours of lecture and demonstration.

title: ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
description: Introduces the student to principles of electronic measurement. Students will gain practical knowledge of both transducers and interface circuitry used in the measurement of physical quantities.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'three hours of lab'}

title: GENERAL PHYSICS LABORATORY II
description: Two-hour laboratory course to accompany PHYS 252.

title: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM I
description: A theoretical treatment of electricity and magnetism.

title: GENERAL PHYSICS FOR SCIENCE MAJORS I
description: For the student in engineering, mathematics, chemistry, or physics. Study of principles and applications of mechanics, heat, and sound. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MATH 221 or consent of the Department

title: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM II
description: A theoretical treatment of electricity and magnetism.

title: LABORATORY WORK FOR SCIENCE MAJORS I
description: Two-hour laboratory course to accompany PHYS 261.

title: ADV LABORATORY I
description: Selected experiments in several branches of physics.

title: GENERAL PHYSICS FOR SCIENCE MAJORS II
description: For the student in engineering, mathematics, chemistry, or physics. Study of principles and applications of light, electricity, and magnetism. Three hours of lecture.

title: ADVANCED LABORATORY II
description: Selected experiments in several branches of physics.

title: LABORATORY WORK FOR SCIENCE MAJORS II
description: Two-hour laboratory course to accompany PHYS 262.

title: ATOMIC PHYSICS
description: A survey of modern physical theories of atomic structure spectra, molecular structure, relativity, and a brief introduction to nuclear physics.
prerequisites: PHYS 365 and PHYS 441 or consent of the Department

title: RESEARCH INTERNSHIP
description: Individual reading and theoretical and/or experimental research on introductory problems in Physics. One hour of research.

title: NUCLEAR PHYSICS
description: A survey of radioactivity, nuclear structure, and elementary particles.
prerequisites: PHYS 365 and PHYS 441 , or consent of the Department

title: RESEARCH INTERNSHIP
description: Individual reading and theoretical and/or experimental research on introductory problems in physics. One hour of research.

title: RESEARCH INTERNSHIP
description: Individual reading and theoretical and/or experimental research on introductory problems in physics. One hour of research.

title: INTRODUCTION TO OPTICS
description: A course in the fundamental principles of optics to include geometrical, physical and modern optics.
prerequisites: PHYS 262 , MATH 223 , and MATH 355 or consent of the Department

title: DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
description: A basic course stressing the properties of integrated circuits such as gates, counters, latches, memories, CPU, etc. The aim of this course is to give the interested student a better understanding of the hardware associated with microprocessors.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'three hours of lab'}

title: INTERMEDIATE ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM I
description: For the student majoring in physics or computer science with option in general physics. An intermediate course in electricity and magnetism covering electrostatic, electromagnetism, and an introduction to Maxwell’s equations.
prerequisites: PHYS 262 , MATH 355 , and credit or concurrent enrollment in MATH 223

title: INTERMEDIATE ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM II
description: A continuation of PHYS 325 covering Maxwell’s equations, electromagnetic waves, electrodynamics and special relativity.

title: INTERMEDIATE MECHANICS
description: Application of the fundamental laws of mechanics to particles and rigid bodies.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MODERN PHYSICS
description: Primarily for physics majors or computer science majors with option in physics. An introduction to special relativity and quantum mechanics.

title: THERMAL PHYSICS
description: An introduction to thermodynamics, statistical mechanics, and kinetic theory.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ASTROPHYSICS
description: An introduction to astrophysics including cosmic rays, their sources, accelerating mechanisms, and interaction with terrestrial and galactic magnetic fields.
prerequisites: PHYS 441 or consent of the Department

title: MODERN PHYSICS
description: A survey of special relativity, quantum mechanics, atomic and nuclear physics. This course is designed for secondary school science teachers.
prerequisites: Two terms of college physics or consent of the Department

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN PHYSICS AND/OR ELECTRONICS
description: Individual reading, conferences, and/or laboratory work on an advanced problem or problems in physics and/or electronics. Students will be responsible for the complete documentation of their assigned problem and its outcome. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of three term hours.

title: INTRODUCTION TO POLITICAL SCIENCE
description: Introduces the applications and approaches to the study of political science. Demonstrates the applications and approaches using examples from comparative politics, international relations, public administration, political behavior, political theory, and other subfields of the discipline.

title: AMERICAN GOVERNMENT
description: A survey of the principles, structure, processes, and functions of American government with emphasis on the national government.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture.', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CPOL 2013/Introduction to American Government'}

title: THE WASHINGTON POLITICAL ARENA
description: An examination of American government and politics in Washington, D.C. Extended visits to private and public institutions are scheduled to bring students into closer contact with the American political system.
others: {'grading': 'Grading is on a pass/no credit basis.', 'seminar_hours': 'Three hours of seminar.'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO PUBLIC ADMINISTRATION
description: A study of the problems and methods of administering public policies with emphasis on the organization and responsiveness of governmental institutions.

title: EUROPEAN POLITICS
description: A survey of European governments, their political cultures, and their formal and informal institutions.
prerequisites: POLI 101 or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture.', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CPOL 2213/Introduction to Comparative Government'}

title: THIRD WORLD POLITICS
description: Examines the conceptual approaches utilized in comparing political systems and explores in case studies the relevance of such approaches and performance of systems in Africa, Asia, the Middle East, Latin America, and the South Pacific.
prerequisites: POLI 101 or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture.', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CPOL 2213/Introduction to Comparative Government'}

title: THE JUDICIAL PROCESS AND THE SUPREME COURT
description: An introduction to the structure and decision-making of the United States judicial system.

title: GOVERNMENT OF LOUISIANA
description: A general survey of state and local government and politics in Louisiana.

title: AMERICAN STATE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENT
description: A survey of the structures, functions, and problems of state and local governments in the United States.

title: QUANTUM MECHANICS I
description: The basic concepts of non-relativistic quantum mechanics, operators and matrices, intrinsic and orbital angular momenta, atomic structure, perturbation theory, and scattering theory. Three hours of lecture.

title: QUANTUM MECHANICS II
description: A continuation of PHYS 475 . Three hours of lecture.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MATHEMATICAL PHYSICS I
description: An introduction to the mathematical treatment of selected physical problems. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: MATH 223 , MATH 254 , and MATH 355 , or consent of the Department

title: INTRODUCTION TO MATHEMATICAL PHYSICS II
description: An introduction to the mathematical treatment of selected physical problems. Three hours of lecture.

title: SELECTED TOPICS IN THEORETICAL PHYSICS
description: Topics to be selected from quantum mechanics, quantum statistics, relativity, relativistic quantum mechanics, quantum field theory, particle physics, and solid-state physics. Three hours of lecture.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN PHYSICS
description: In-depth study of special areas in physics. May be repeated when topic varies. One to three hours of lecture.

title: SPECIAL PROBLEMS IN PHYSICS AND/OR ELECTRONICS
description: Individual reading, conference presentations, and/or laboratory work on an advanced problem or project in physics and/or electronics. Three hours of independent work per week are required per credit hour. Course may be repeated for a maximum total of 9 credit hours.

title: MODERN PHYSICS
description: A survey of special relativity, quantum mechanics, atomic and nuclear physics and the resources available to support teaching or these topics. This course is designed for secondary school science teachers. Three hours or lecture.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MICROPROCESSORS
description: This course is especially designed as in-service for high school physics teachers. A hands-on learning experience with microprocessors, specifically the Intel 8085, for control and applications in such areas as data acquisition storage and retrieval and process control. The emphasis is on learning to interface a microprocessor to devices, systems, instruments, and machines. Three hours of lecture and three hours of lab.

title: ELECTRONICS INSTRUMENTATION
description: This course is intended for high school physics teachers. Students will gain practical knowledge of both transducers and interface circuitry used in the measurement of physical quantities. Three hours of lecture and three hours of lab.

title: URBAN POLITICS
description: An examination of politics in urban America, emphasizing the impact of forms of municipal and county government, public officials, social and economic sites, political parties, factions, interest groups, and the general public upon policy-making in urban communities.

title: POLITICAL PARTIES AND INTEREST GROUPS
description: A study of political parties, interest groups, and campaigning in the United States.

title: LEGISLATIVE POLITICS
description: A study of the organization, functions, and behavior of legislative systems in the United States with a special emphasis on the United States Congress.

title: PRESIDENTAL POLITICS
description: An examination of the office of the President, its roles and functions. Includes policy formation and a study of the organization and function of the executive branch.

title: RESEARCH METHODS IN THE SOCIAL SCIENCES
description: This course presents an overview of the basic methods and techniques used in social sciences research. It includes descriptive and inferential statistics, hypothesis testing, population and same identification, data collection, statistical analysis, and research report presentation with special emphasis on the areas of criminal justice, political science, and sociology.
cross_listed: Also listed as CJ 330 , NPA 330 and SOCL 330 .

title: PRACTICUM IN NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Explores strategic planning, marketing, legal issues, program assessment, fundraising, development, financial planning, risk management, social media, human resources management, and organizational communication within nonprofit organizations and the particular challenges involved differing from for-profit organizations.
cross_listed: Also listed as NPA 392 and SOCL 392 .

title: FIELD RESEARCH IN GOVERNMENT AND LAW
description: Students will be placed in the work environment of various governmental and legal offices. Only one placement may be in the State of Louisiana and one in the Washington, D.C. area. Grading is on a pass/no credit basis. Minimum of nine hours of research per week. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: POLITICAL BEHAVIOR
description: A study of individual political behavior, focusing on political psychology and communication, dealing with acquisition and dissemination of attitudes about politics, participation, issue preferences, and ideological and partisan attachments.

title: AMERICAN CONSTITUTIONAL LAW
description: A study of the development of the United States Constitution emphasizing the role of the Supreme Court in interpreting the Constitution. Attention is devoted to the relationships and powers of the branches of the national government and to the civil liberties.

title: UNITED STATES FOREIGN POLICY IN THE 20TH CENTURY
description: A study of American foreign policy from the World War II period to the present with emphasis on the foreign policy making process.
prerequisites: POLI 101 or consent of the Department

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the guidance of a member of the Political Science faculty. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of research.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN POLITICAL SCIENCE
description: Special topics are selected from current problems in political sciences. Topics vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: INTRODUCTION TO PSYCHOLOGY
description: An introductory survey course that introduces students to the basic concepts and science of psychology. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.

title: EDUCATIONAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: Applications of psychology to the educative process. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.

title: CHILD PSYCHOLOGY
description: A study of the physical, social, and cognitive development of the child. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.

title: ADOLESCENT PSYCHOLOGY
description: Consideration of adolescent behavior in light of information available on social, physical, and psychological development. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.

title: LIFESPAN DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: A study of growth and socio-emotional development throughout the life span, with emphasis on fundamental principles of development. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.

title: SOCIAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: Survey of individual behavior and thought in social situations. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.

title: INTRODUCTORY STATISTICS FOR THE BEHAVIORAL SCIENCES
description: The course introduces students to basic statistical theories and techniques used in the behavioral sciences. Topics include descriptive statistics, statistical inference, correlation and regression, “t” test, chi-square test, and analysis of variance. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.
prerequisites: Three hours of college mathematics at MATH 121 or higher

title: INQUIRY AND WRITING IN PSYCHOLOGY
description: An introduction to scholarly literature and writing in psychological science. Students will learn how to find and critically evaluate psychological research, discriminate between models of psychological literature, write using appropriate style, and communicate conclusions effectively in written format. Three hours of lecture or equivalent.

title: ABNORMAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: An introduction to psychopathological processes from the historical and clinical perspectives, including the foundations of diagnosis in relationship to causation and treatment.

title: THE PSYCHOLOGY OF EXCEPTIONALITY
description: The etiology, nature, and prognosis of mental retardation, learning disabilities, and other exceptionalities. A presentation of the basic research and theoretical work, pertaining to the psychological aspects of exceptionality with special emphasis on the identification and remediation of learning disabilities.

title: HISTORY OF MODERN PSYCHOLOGY
description: A historical survey of psychology with special reference to schools of psychology.

title: PHYSIOLOGICAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: An introduction to the functioning of the nervous system with respect to sensation, perception, learning, and motivation.

title: THE NATURE AND CONDITIONS OF HUMAN SEXUAL RESPONSE
description: A review of the psychological, sociological, and physiological components, and the dynamic interaction of these forces in shaping human sexual response. Related topics to be considered include sex education, the relationship between sex and love, marital adjustment, and parenthood.

title: THE PSYCHOLOGY OF ADDICTION
description: A review of the field of addiction with special emphasis on personality dynamics of the addict and the emotional, behavioral, and physiological effects of substance abuse.

title: ETHICAL, LEGAL, AND PROFESSIONAL ISSUES IN PSYCHOLOGY
description: A review of the ethical principles, standards, and guidelines governing the mental health disciplines, with a primary focus on the practice of psychology. Also included are the essential legal issues and mandates as these relate to professional ethics.
prerequisites: Minimum of 9 hours of Psychology, or consent of the Department

title: INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: An overview of the practice, techniques, roles, and ethics of clinical psychology.
prerequisites: Senior standing or consent of the Department

title: PSYCHOLOGY OF DEATH AND DYING
description: A review of psychological factors relating to death and dying, death attitudes, understanding bereavement, and constructive ways of coping with death and dying.

title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS
description: An overview of the origins, roles and structures of non-state agents in international politics, including their interactions in the areas of conflict, diplomacy and law, security arrangements, and economic and other forms of integration.
prerequisites: POLI 101 or consent of the Department

title: INTRODUCTION TO HEALTH PSYCHOLOGY
description: An overview of health psychology, including principles of behavioral medicine, physiological foundation and the management of health-related behaviors.

title: WESTERN POLITICAL THOUGHT
description: A study of some of the writings of classical, medieval, Renaissance, and modern political theorists and their contribution to western political ideas.
prerequisites: POLI 101 or consent of the Department

title: PUBLIC POLICY AND EVALUATION
description: Presents a survey of public policy making with particular attention to program evaluation methods and the role of evaluation in public policy making.

title: JURISPRUDENCE
description: An investigation into how the law describes the relationship between individuals and the state and society, and the appropriateness of law’s restrictions upon individual behavior.
prerequisites: POLI 101 or consent of the Department

title: INTERNATIONAL POLITICS
description: Analysis of international relations as a political process, concluding with a review of contemporary international problems.
prerequisites: POLI 101 or consent of the Department

title: POLITICS IN THE CINEMA
description: Investigates how political communication occurs through film, the messages sent through film, and the influence of film in shaping or altering political attitudes.

title: SEMINAR IN POLITICAL SCIENCE
description: Selected topics from current problems in political science. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: SEMINAR IN NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Designed as a preparatory for student internships in not-for-profit organizations by having them solve problems in areas such as finances, personnel, marketing, and risk management in a simulated organization. There will be individual/group projects.
cross_listed: Also listed as NPA 492 and SOCL 492 .

title: AMERICAN HUMANICS MANAGEMENT TRAINING INSTITUTE
description: Participation in the annual meeting of the American Humanics Management Training Institute. Funds for attendance are to be raised by the student.

title: INDEPENT STUDIES
description: Readings, conferences and reports under the direction of a member of the political science faculty. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: POLITICAL BEHAVIOR
description: A study of individual political behavior, focusing on political psychology and communication, dealing with acquisition and dissemination of attitudes about politics, participation, issue preferences, and ideological and partisan attachments. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: AMERICAN CONSTITUTIONAL LAW
description: A study of the development of the United States Constitution emphasizing the role of the Supreme Court in interpreting the Constitution. Attention is devoted to the relationships and powers of the branches of the national government and to the civil liberties. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: UNITED STATES FOREIGN POLICY IN THE 20TH CENTURY
description: A socio-cultural analysis of American foreign policy from 1900 to the present with emphasis on World War I, World War II, Korea, Vietnam, and the Cold War. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS
description: An overview of the origins, roles and structures of non-state agents in international politics. Students will analyze competing theories of interaction in areas such as diplomacy, law, and conflict. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: WESTERN POLITICAL THOUGHT
description: An analysis of classical, medieval, Renaissance, and modern political theorists and their contribution to Western political ideas. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: PUBLIC POLICY EVALUATION
description: Survey of the history and development of program evaluation methods with an analysis of theories of the role of evaluation in public policy-making. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: JUDISPRUDENCE
description: An investigation into various theories of how law describes the individual’s relationship with the state and society, including a socio- cultural analysis of law’s restrictions upon individual behavior. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: INTERNATIONAL POLITICS
description: Analysis of the theories of international relations as a political process, and their application to contemporary international problems. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: POLITICS IN THE CINEMA
description: Investigates how political communication occurs through film, the messages sent through film, and the influence of film in shaping or altering political attitudes. Leadership of discussions involving intense investigation and explication of several films is required. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: SEMINAR IN POLITICAL SCIENCE
description: Selected topics from current problems in political science. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: EVALUATING PRACTICE IN APPLIED BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: An overview of single-subject experimental designs and using science in treatment and research.
prerequisites: PSYC 255 or consent of the Department

title: FIELD EXPERIENCE IN ABA
description: Supervised experience in a program-approved behavior analysis field placement setting.
prerequisites: PSYC 255 and consent of the Department
others: {'field_experience_hours': 'Ten hours of field experience each week', 'repeatability': 'May be repeated for up to 12 credits'}

title: INDEPENDENT READING AND RESEARCH IN PSYCHOLOGY
description: The individual student is responsible for securing the guidance of a qualified psychology Department faculty member, proposing a formal course of study approved by the faculty and selecting the area of reading or research.
prerequisites: Senior standing and consent of the Department

title: PSYCHOLOGY FIELD EXPERIENCE
description: NONE
prerequisites: Senior standing, PSYC 430 , availability of an appropriate placement, and consent of the Department
others: {'lab_hours': 'Nine hours of lab', 'repeatability': 'May not be repeated'}

title: SEMINAR IN PSYCHOLOGY
description: Selected topics from current issues and problems in psychology. May be repeated as long as the topics are different for a maximum of nine credit hours.
prerequisites: Senior standing and consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture or equivalent', 'repeatability': 'May be repeated as long as the topics are different for a maximum of nine credit hours'}

title: DISPUTE RESOLUTION STRATEGIES
description: An introduction to specific programs and activities designed to prevent and reduce violence. Emphasis on creating cooperative learning environments where students learn negotiation and peer meditation strategies.
prerequisites: PSYC 152 or PSYC 206 and senior standing

title: PERSONALITY
description: Determinants and dynamics of personality and research theory.

title: COUNSELING EXCEPTIONAL CHILDREN
description: An introduction to the counseling techniques and procedures appropriate for counseling exceptional children and their parents.

title: THE PSYCHOLOGY OF EXCEPTIONALITY
description: Critical thinking and problem solving applied to basic research and theoretical work pertaining to the psychological aspects of exceptionality with a special emphasis on the identification and remediation of learning disabilities.
prerequisites: Graduate standing or consent of the Department

title: HISTORY OF MODERN PSYCHOLOGY
description: A historical survey of the development of major schools of modern psychology. Students will conduct a critical analysis of a major psychologist who has made a significant contribution to one of these schools. Results of this research will be presented in an oral presentation.

title: THE NATURE AND CONDITIONS OF HUMAN SEXUAL RESPONSE
description: A review of the psychological, sociological, and physiological components, and the dynamic interaction of these forces in shaping human sexual response. Related topics to be considered include sex education, the relationship between sex and love, marital adjustment, and parenthood.

title: THE PSYCHOLOGY OF ADDICITION
description: A review of the field of addiction including an overview of the agents most frequently abused, with special emphasis on personality dynamics of the addict and the behavioral and physiological effects of substance abuse.

title: INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: An overview of the practice, techniques, and ethics of clinical psychology.

title: PSYCHOLOGY OF DEATH AND DYING
description: A review of psychological factors relating to death and dying, death attitudes, understanding bereavement, and constructive ways of coping with death and dying.

title: INTRODUCTION TO HEALTH PSYCHOLOGY
description: An overview of the field of Health Psychology including biopsychosocial approach, physiological foundations, and management of health- related behaviors. Students will demonstrate critical thinking by researching an important topic in health and giving a formal presentation to the class.

title: PRACTICUM IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: Supervised experience in a program-approved behavior analysis field placement setting. Ten hours of field experience each week. Cannot be repeated.
others: {'field_experience_hours': 'Ten hours of field experience each week', 'repeatability': 'Cannot be repeated'}

title: INTERNSHIP IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS I
description: Supervised experience in a program-approved field placement setting. Can be repeated.

title: INTERNSHIP IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS II
description: Supervised experience in a program-approved field placement setting. Can be repeated.

title: INDEPENDENT READING AND RESEARCH IN PSYCHOLOGY
description: Students must 1) develop a clear written proposal for an intensive readings review or research in conjunction with a Department faculty mentor, 2) submit the written proposal for approval by the entire Department faculty, and then, 3) write an in-depth and comprehensive project summary for review by the full Department faculty upon completion.
prerequisites: Graduate standing and consent of the Department

title: FIELD EXPERIENCE
description: Must involve a formal written proposal, an appropriate Department faculty sponsor, appropriate placement and supervision. Training, experiential, and outcome goals must be Department-approved a priori, and their realization will serve as the basis of performance evaluation.
prerequisites: Graduate Standing and consent of the Department

title: SEMINAR IN PSYCHOLOGY
description: Most frequently, these seminars are preliminary offerings of 700-level courses under development and involve in-depth and critical analysis of a specialized topic in psychological science.
prerequisites: Graduate Standing and consent of the Department

title: DISPUTE RESOLUTION STRATEGIES
description: An introduction to specific programs and activities designed to prevent and reduce violence. Emphasis on creating cooperative learning environments where students learn negotiation and peer meditation strategies.

title: PLAY THERAPY WITH CHILDREN
description: A non-directive approach to psychotherapy with children (ages 4 to 10). Emphasis will be on play media techniques, the symbolic nature of child’s play, and the emotional life of children in their unique social/family contexts.

title: ADVANCED PSYCHOPATHOLOGY
description: Detailed study of psychopathology and the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM) of the American Psychiatric Association. Extensive readings outside of the DSM-5 are also required.
prerequisites: Undergraduate personality theory course or consent of the Department

title: PRINCIPLES AND THEORIES OF LEARNING
description: A review of principles and theories of learning, the research on which they are based, and their application to the teaching/learning process and psychotherapy.

title: ADVANCED CHILD PSYCHOLOGY
description: A presentation of research methodology, theory, and data on child development and behavior.

title: ADVANCED ADOLESCENT PSYCHOLOGY
description: Personality dynamics and development in adolescence; adaptive and maladaptive reactions to the developmental tasks and cultural pressures of adolescence.

title: ADVANCED HUMAN DEVELOPMENT
description: A survey of human development across the lifespan through the integration of theories and current research to study physical, cognitive, and social-emotional development.

title: INTRODUCTION TO SCHOOL PSYCHOLOGY
description: A survey of the professional field of school psychology covering training, practice, professional issues, research, consultation, assessment, history, and scope of the profession today. Emphasis on ethical standards guiding practice of school psychology.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture', 'field_experience_hours': '20 hours of field experience'}

title: CHILD AND ADOLESCENT PSYCHOPATHOLOGY
description: A comprehensive presentation of diagnostic classification systems for child and adolescent psychopathology. Topics include prevalent theories regarding the causes of disorders, assessment strategies, and evidence-based treatment models including psychopharmacological interventions.
prerequisites: Graduate standing or consent of the Department

title: PSYCHOLOGY OF THE MILD/MODERATE HANDICAPPED CHILD
description: The etiology, nature, and prognosis of the mild/moderate exceptional child; discussion of specific behavior problems, therapy and management of the mild/moderate; role of social and other agencies in the education of the mild/moderate; trends in theory and practice.

title: INTRODUCTION TO APPLIED BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: An introduction of basic principles of behavior, intervention procedures, measurement and assessment tools, and ethics used in the field by applied behavior analysis practitioners. The content in this course fulfills the 40 hour training requirement for eligibility to apply to become a Registered Behavior Technician (RBT).

title: PSYCHOLOGY OF ADJUSTMENT
description: An introduction to the study of adjustment mechanisms in normal individuals, plus a survey presentation of broad areas of abnormal behavior and the major personality theorists.

title: PSYCHOLOGY RESEARCH METHODS
description: Introduction to the scientific method of psychology, including experimental designs, measurements, analysis, and ethics in psychological research. Emphasis on learning in critically evaluate research reports, applying the experimental method, and developing skills for preparing written proposals according to APA style conventions.
prerequisites: Introductory statistics or consent of the Department

title: TEST AND MEASUREMENT
description: Included are categorizing psychological tests, along with their importance. Ethical considerations and use with special populations will also be discussed; statistical interpretation of scores will be reviewed. Reliability and validity will be discussed in detail, along with how to interpret them and make decisions. Information as to development and piloting tests will also be offered. Survey data, as well as the use of psychological tests in education, clinical counseling, and organizational settings will be discussed.
prerequisites: Introductory statistics course, or consent of the Department

title: BEHAVIORAL OBSERVATION AND ASSESSMENT
description: An overview of behavioral assessment procedures, including structural analyses, functional behavior assessment, task analysis, and curriculum-based assessments.
prerequisites: PSYC 255 or consent of the Department

title: APPLICATIONS OF APPLIED BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: A review of the principles and procedures of interventions in applied behavior analysis with children and adolescents to promote skill development and reduce interfering behaviors.
prerequisites: PSYC 255 or consent of the Department

title: COGNITIVE PSYCHOLOGY
description: An introduction to theories and research related to human cognition. Memory, attention, thinking, language, and metacognition are emphasized.

title: CHILD PSYCHOPATHOLOGY
description: The etiology, nature, and prognosis of psychopathology in children; discussion of specific behavior problems; therapy and management of the maladjusted; role of social and other agencies in the education of the maladjusted, trends in theory and practice.

title: PERSONALITY
description: A study of determinants and dynamics of personality theory.

title: THERAPEUTIC INTERVENTIONS FOR CHILDREN AND YOUTH
description: Current theory, research, and application in providing cognitive-behavioral interventions for children and youth with academic, social, emotional, and behavioral difficulties. Guidance for case conceptualization, counseling techniques, progress monitoring, and intervention decision-making with school professionals and multi-disciplinary teams.

title: PROGRAM PLANNING AND EVALUATION
description: A study of the issues, models, and process of program planning and evaluation in educational and mental health settings.

title: INTERMEDIATE STATISTICS
description: The course offers coverage of computational procedures and basic theories in statistics to prepare graduate students for data analysis and interpretation in psychology. The topics include tests for comparing means and proportions, confidence intervals, simple and partial correlation, simple and multiple regressions, and nonparametric tests.
prerequisites: Three hours of basic statistics or consent of the Department

title: ADVANCED EXPERIMENTAL PSYCHOLOGY
description: Supervised research in general experimental psychology. The selection, design, execution, analysis, and reporting of the psychological experiment.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture', 'lab_hours': 'two hours of lab'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO METHODOLOGY AND RESEARCH DESIGN
description: An overview of the literature dealing with the scientific approach to research design and methodology.
prerequisites: Three hours of basic statistics or consent of the Department

title: PHYSIOLOGICAL PSYCHOLOGY AND BEHAVIORAL NEUROSCIENCE
description: The course is an advanced study of the relationship between physiological functions and human behavior. The class will draw heavily from neuroscience and neuro-imaging technology.

title: SINGLE SUBJECT EXPERIMENTAL DESIGNS
description: Theory and skills on the use and analysis of single case research designs in applied settings. Single-subject, or intra-subject, analysis is a primary experimental research methodology in applied behavior analysis, special education, and other related human service fields. It is tied closely to individual intervention methodologies that characterize effective, more intensive service delivery.

title: ORGANIZATIONAL BEHAVIOR MANAGEMENT
description: Current research and theory on the provision of supervision to human services personnel in behavior-analytic and educational settings, and evidence-based methods of skill development and staff management.

title: APPLIED TECHNIQUES IN FAMILY THERAPY
description: Application of marriage and family therapy techniques, including conjoint and inter-generational therapies.
prerequisites: Marriage and Family Therapy course, or consent of the Department

title: PSYCHOPHARMACOLOGY FOR PRACTICIONERS
description: The course provides a review of chemical neurotransmission before focusing on medications used for the treatment of psychological disorders in children, adolescents, and adults.

title: BEHAVIORAL PEDIATRICS
description: Introduction to research-based practices in providing psychological services to children and adolescents who exhibit behavior problems that occur across home, school, and recreational settings. Special emphasis is placed on community awareness and prevention.

title: CASE FORMULATION
description: This course is designed to consolidate previous learning experiences related to assessment procedures, data integration, case conceptualization, treatment planning, and assessment report writing.

title: ETHICS AND PROFESSIONALISM FOR BEHAVIORAL SERVICES
description: This graduate seminar is a survey of ethical, legal, and professional issues facing school psychologists and behavior analysts. It is designed to teach a process of ethical decision-making and to increase awareness of the complexities in practice in accordance with BACB, APA, and NASP guidelines.

title: PSYCHO-EDUCATIONAL ASSESSMENT
description: Designed to provide the student with expertise in the assessment of achievement of school-aged children, and with knowledge of assessment instruments. Opportunities are presented for administering, scoring, and interpreting tests and writing integrated psychological reports.

title: PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT AND PRACTICUM I
description: Training in all phases of cognitive and psycho-educational assessment and report writing with an emphasis on cognitive and social areas of assessment.
prerequisites: Admission to candidacy in the School Psychology program or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture': 'Three hours of lecture', 'lab': 'three hours of lab'}

title: PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT II
description: Course involves the presentation of a variety of objective and projective tests of personality.
others: {'lecture': 'Three hours of lecture', 'lab': 'one hour of lab'}

title: PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT PRACTIUM I
description: Supervised practice in cognitive assessment techniques including administration of major instruments, test interpretation, and report writing. Special emphasis will be placed on professional, legal, and ethical practice.

title: PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT PRACTIUM II
description: Supervised practice in the field of School Psychology, including consultation with school/community personnel, counseling, behavioral/academic intervention CBA, referral process, and multi-disciplinary team responsibilities with an emphasis on best practices within the school setting.

title: APPLIED BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: Current research and applications of behavioral principles to children in school and clinical settings. Emphasis on techniques, practical, ethical, and legal considerations, and selecting appropriate interventions. Prevention and intervention strategies will be covered primarily in an applied format.
prerequisites: Graduate standing or consent of the Department

title: APPLIED BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS IN AUTISM
description: The study of the etiology, nature, and core deficits presented in Autism spectrum disorder and related disabilities. A review and critical examination of behavioral interventions applicable to school and behavior-analytic services related to each core deficit.

title: BEHAVIOR NEUROSCIENCE
description: An overview of the neurophysiological, neurochemical, and neuroanatomical influences on behavior.

title: SCIENCE AND PHILOSOPHY OF BEHAVIOR
description: This course will examine behaviorism, the underlying philosophy of behavior analysis, as well as concepts, implications, and applications of that philosophy.

title: BEHAVIORAL ASESSMENT AND INTERVENTION
description: Current research and theory on using data to identify empirically valid and clinically indicated interventions for behavioral and academic prevention and remediation efforts, and developing and implementing effective progress-monitoring practices for selected interventions.

title: COMPLEX BEHAVIOR
description: This course will provide an in-depth examination of the nature of behavior, from complex phenomena observed in the basic principles of behavior to those which are unique (theoretically) to human organisms such as language and cognition.

title: ADVANCED FUNCTIONAL ASSESSMENT AND INTERVENTION
description: This course is designed to provide an examination of current functional assessment methods including indirect methods, descriptive assessment, functional analysis, and current modifications and extensions of functional assessment approaches.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: Examination of systems, interventions, current issues, and/or advances in applied behavior analysis. Topics vary.

title: CONSULTATION IN HUMAN DEVELOPMENT SETTINGS
description: Study of models, processes, and issues of consultation and interpersonal communication in settings where human development needs and crises are managed by professionals seeking aid from psychologists.

title: INTERNSHIP IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS I
description: Supervised intermediate experience in a program-approved behavior analysis field placement setting. Twenty hours of field experience each week. Cannot be repeated.

title: INTERNSHIP IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS II
description: Supervised advanced experience in a program-approved behavior analysis field placement setting. Twenty hours of field experience each week. Can be repeated.

title: INTERNSHIP IN SCHOOL PSYCHOLOGY I
description: Supervised on-the-job internship in school psychology. Students will be assigned to program-approved field setting which emphasizes basic applied experiences in school psychology. Two hours of seminar and thirty hours of lab.
prerequisites: PSYC 754 and successful completion of the comprehensive examination leading to the Specialist of School Psychology degree
others: {'seminar_hours': 'Two hours of seminar', 'lab_hours': 'thirty hours of lab'}

title: PROGRAM PLANNING
description: A comprehensive study and application of established models for planning, implementing, and evaluating small and large-scale health programs. Includes an introduction to the evaluation of community-based public health programs.

title: PRINCIPLES OF EPIDEMIOLOGY
description: An introduction to the skills needed by public health professionals to critically interpret the epidemiologic literature. Discusses measures of frequency and association, introduce the design and validity of epidemiologic research, and give an overview of data analysis.

title: PRINCIPLES OF BIOSTATISTICS
description: A focus on statistical analysis in public health and health services administration. Topics include distributions, tests of significance and confidence levels, sample situations with special attention to those occurring in health education, and behavioral sciences.

title: ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH
description: A review of environmental risk factors of disease. Topics include community air and water pollution, occupational health, hazardous waste, pesticides and food radiation protection including approaches to assessing, controlling, and preventing these hazards.

title: SOCIAL AND BEHAVIORAL SCIENCES IN PUBLIC HEALTH
description: This course will provide the student with concepts of community health education, health promotion and disease prevention; their association with social, behavioral, and physical sciences in relation to health maintenance, optimal health, disease prevention.

title: U.S. HEALTH CARE INDUSTRY
description: A review of health policy and management issues and their impact on organizing, financing, and delivery of public health and medical delivery systems.

title: EMPOWERMENT THROUGH COMMUNITY HEALTH SCIENCES
description: An introduction to the theory and practice of community organizations, including models and strategies of community organization and their application to health problems and health policy.

title: RISK ASSESSMENT
description: An overview of public health risk assessment associated with chemical exposures. Topics covered include an overview of chemical exposures, an introduction to the concepts, principles, and evaluation strategies used in risk assessment, the risk assessment framework, and development of public health risk management strategies.

title: CONFLICT RESOLUTION
description: An examination of conflict theories and alternative dispute resolution (ADR) mechanisms (negotiation, mediation, and arbitration) public health professionals may employ to resolve disputes and conflicts arising in public health practice.

title: PUBLIC HEALTH STRATEGIC PLANNING
description: A review of strategic planning for public health program delivery; exploring such topics as vision and mission, program design, including goals and objectives, basic assumptions, and program evaluations including design of outcome measurements and benchmarking.

title: INTERNSHIP IN SCHOOL PSYCHOLOGY II
description: Students will be assigned to program-approved field setting which emphasizes advanced experiences in school psychology. Two hours of seminar and thirty hours of lab. A continuation of PSYC 788 . Supervised on-the-job internship in school psychology.

title: PROJECT IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: Research or clinical project conducted under the mentorship of a faculty member. Taken for 1-6 credits per semester. Can be repeated.

title: THESIS IN BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS
description: Master’s Thesis conducted under the mentorship of a faculty member. Taken for 1-6 credits per semester. Can be repeated.

title: THESIS IN PSYCHOLOGY
description: Available for six credits toward a graduate degree in psychology. May not be substituted for required courses in the degree program. From one to six credits per term.
prerequisites: Consent of the Department after being admitted to candidacy for a Psychology degree

title: INTRODUCTION TO PUBLIC HEALTH AND HEALTH DISPARITIES
description: An introduction to the history of public health as well as the scope of services, politics, and budget of health care agencies at the federal, state, and local levels; evaluate the value and the implications of diversity (racial, ethnic, cultural, economic, and social) and the importance of cultural competency in addressing public health issues.

title: PUBLIC HEALTH LAW AND ETHICS
description: An examination of both the legal powers and ethical perspectives and duties of the state that exist to assure the conditions for people to be healthy and the limits on that power to constrain the autonomy privacy, liberty, propriety, or other legally protected interests of individuals.

title: PUBLIC HEALTH LEADERSHIP AND ETHICS
description: A review of concepts and theories of leadership, to present challenges from public health practices and research, and to discover personal leadership attributes. The content will include leadership theory as it relates to the person, organization, communities, and research. Incorporated within this course will be an examination of ethical perspectives and duties of the state that exist to assure the conditions for people to be healthy and the limits on the power to constrain the autonomy, privacy, liberty, propriety, or other legally protected interest of individuals.

title: HUMAN DISEASE AND HEALTH PROMOTION FOR PUBLIC HEALTH PROFESSIONALS
description: A review of current research and theory from molecular biology that has important implications for the current and future approach to public health. Provides understanding of the potential applications of genetic engineering to their health specialties.

title: PRINCIPLES OF HEALTH PROMOTION AND INTERVENTION
description: An overview of current issues and research associated with health promotion in the community, including the workplace and focusing on implementation and evaluation of the health promotion programs.

title: PUBLIC HEALTH NUTRITION
description: An introduction to the basic nutritional needs for public health programs with examination of the role of diet health, and disease throughout the population, including weight control and fitness issues.

title: GLOBAL HEALTH
description: An introduction to public health theory and practice in the context of developing countries. Topics include public health methods for analyzing the causes and patterns of disease in populations and the types of health concerns in developing countries.

title: BIOPSYCHOSOCIAL MODEL OF HEALTH AND HEALTH CARE
description: This course is designed to be an introductory course to offer insight into the biological, psychological and social influences on health, with an emphasis on the application of biopsychosocial principles upon disease onset, progression and management. Coverage will include psychosocial theory and research on: 1) models of health and disease, 2) treatment and management of chronic disease (e.g., cancer, cardiovascular disease, HIV/AIDS), 3) preventing disease and other threats to health (e.g., smoking cessation, exercise and weight management), and 4) the association of public health to related fields including medicine.

title: GEOGRAPHIC INFORMATION SYSTEMS (GIS) IN PUBLIC HEALTH
description: The application of GIS in their public health career. Topics covered include mapping, development of geographical datasets, and data analysis from application of GIS in different disciplines of public health.

title: RESEARCH METHODS
description: An overview of qualitative and quantitative research methods and techniques used in designing and conducting health research of community-based public health programs.

title: PROGRAM EVALUATION AND ANALYSIS
description: This course will focus on dimensions of practical health program evaluations with emphasis on meeting the needs of non-profit and for-profit agencies, program administrators, and program planners.

title: UNDERSTANDING POVERTY - A PUBLIC HEALTH PERSPECTIVE
description: This course examines the experiences of living in poverty, middle class, and wealth. The driving force of generational poverty as the cause and possible solution will be presented. Public health’s role in addressing poverty along with possible solutions will be discussed.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF MATERNAL AND CHILD HEALTH
description: Foundations of Maternal and Child Health (MCH) focuses on addressing the health needs of women, children, and families within the broader context of public health. To equip students with the knowledge and skills necessary to promote the health and well-being of mothers, infants, and children through a public health lens.

title: DATA MANAGEMENT AND ANALYSIS IN HEALTH SCIENCES
description: This course will offer comprehensive understanding of four key elements: data management, statistical analysis, graphics, and reproducible reporting. The course is designed for advanced level health sciences students interested in management and analysis of health care data. By the end of the course, students will be able to create, import, clean and manage a dataset, perform basis analyses and visualize results, create customized graphics, and write dynamic reports. The course will use STATA as the analytical software.

title: APPLIED PRACTICE EXPERIENCE (APE)
description: Culminating experience course, also referred to as Internship, in which students conduct supervised fieldwork in a public health-practice setting under the guidance of a mentor to apply the knowledge and skills acquired throughout the curriculum. Students are required to (1) complete 150 hours of fieldwork as part of an internship experience, and (2) produce two projects that demonatrate competence in public health.
prerequisites: PUBH 701 , PUBH 703 , PUBH 707 , PUBH 708 , PUBH 709 , PUBH 712 , PUBH 713 and PUBH 723 unless otherwise approved by the Department.
others: {'grading': 'Grading will be on a pass/fail basis.'}

title: INTEGRATED LEARNING EXPERIENCE (ILE)
description: A written research project under the supervision of a faculty committee. The project is written in traditional academic style or in a journal manuscript format.
others: {'grading': 'Grading will be on a pass/fail basis.'}

title: APPLIED PRACTICE EXPERIENCE (APE)
description: Culminating experience course in which students conduct supervised fieldwork in a public health-practice setting under the guidance of a mentor to apply the knowledge and skills acquired throughout the curriculum. Students are required to (1) complete 150 hours of fieldwork as part of an internship experience, and (2) produce two projects that demonstrate competence in public health. To complete the requirements, students must enroll in and pass three individual one-credit APE courses. Students may choose to enroll in one APE course (1 credit) in the first seven-week session and two concurrent APE courses (2 credits) in the following seven-week session; OR three consecutive APE courses (1 credit each) across three seven-week sessions. Grading will be on a pass/fail basis.
prerequisites: PUBH 701 , PUBH 703 , PUBH 707 , PUBH 708 , PUBH 709 , PUBH 712 , PUBH 713 , and PUBH 723 unless otherwise approved by the Department.

title: INTEGRATED LEARNING EXPERIENCE (ILE) - I
description: A written research project under the supervision of a faculty committee. The project is written in traditional style or in a journal manuscript format. May be repeated up to three times. Grading will be on a pass/fail basis.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS
description: In-depth study of special areas in public health. May be repeated when topic varies. One to three hours of lecture.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY
