description: Independent Study is a course designed for students to pursue special topics of interest as deemed academically sound by the MPH program. This may entail readings, special assignments, and/or research under the guidance of a faculty mentor. May be repeated for a maximum of three credit hours.

title: THESIS I
description: Provides students with knowledge and skills to develop research questions, conduct literature review, plan for data collection, and develop a thesis proposal. Grading will be on a pass/no credit basis.

title: THESIS II
description: Enables students to apply methods of research to the study of public health issues culminating in a final thesis submission. Grading will be on a pass/no credit basis.

title: HISTORY OF CHRISTIANITY I
description: This class is a survey course designed to provide an overview of the history of Christianity and Christian thought from the first century through the middle of the sixteenth century. In addition to providing a basic narrative history of Christianity in the West during this period, the course will involve students in exploring the thoughts of Patristic and Medieval scholars such as Origen, Augustine, and Aquinas. Three hours of lecture.
cross_listed: Also listed as HIST 325 , which cannot be taken for credit in addition to this course.

title: HISTORY OF CHRISTIANITY II
description: This course is a survey of the history of Christianity from the Reformation to the present. This course emphasizes the Reformation and Counter-Reformation, the Enlightenment, and the rise of both liberalism and fundamentalism. This course focuses on the development of Christian ideas and institutions within their corresponding social, cultural, and political contexts. Three hours of lecture.
cross_listed: Also listed as HIST 326 , which cannot be taken for credit in addition to this course.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN THE OLD TESTAMENT
description: A variable topics course for advanced students interested in studying an Old Testament text more deeply than regular offerings permit. Each course will focus on a particular genre, author, interpretation, book, or literary critical approach. May be repeated once for a maximum of six term-hours credit. Three hours of lecture.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN THE NEW TESTAMENT
description: A variable topics course for advanced students interested in studying a New Testament text more deeply than regular offerings permit. It will focus on a particular genre, author, interpretation, book, or literary critical approach. May be repeated once for a maximum of six term-hours credit. Three hours of lecture.

title: ISSUES IN SCIENCE SEMINAR
description: An examination of some of most important recent advances in science and technology and potential impacts.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN SCIENCE
description: Special topics in Science. May be repeated as topics vary from semester to semester up to six hours.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDY IN SCIENCE
description: Independent study in Science. An independent project or study directed by an assigned faculty member.

title: THESIS
description: Research project carried out under the supervision of assigned faculty member. May be repeated.

title: INTRODUCTION TO SOCIOLOGY
description: The study of differing patterns of human relationships: analysis of group structure and social organization; application of sociological concepts to the understanding of human social behavior.

title: GENDER, RACE, AND CLASS IN SOCIETY
description: The study of sub-cultural differences between males and females and between various racial and ethnic groups, of the transmission of cultural differences from generation to generation, and of the perpetuation of class, gender, and racial differences by social institutions.

title: CONTEMPORARY SOCIAL PROBLEMS
description: An analysis of current social problems, including delinquency and crime, family and community disorganization, mental illness, and intergroup relations.

title: INTRODUCTION TO NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: An exploration of the development, trends, and current issues of voluntary and human service organizations with special emphasis on the purpose and scope of the nonprofit sector as it relates to government and the business sector in the United States. Classroom material and skills will be reinforced through participation in service learning.

title: URBAN SOCIOLOGY
description: The rise of the city; development and social structure of urban communities; the ecology and culture of metropolitan areas; the impact of the city on human behavior.

title: MARRIAGE AND FAMILY
description: The study of married and family life as a social institution: the social significance of the family; and the family in transition.

title: MINORITIES IN AMERICA
description: A survey of selected minority groups in the United States and an examination of relations between dominant and minority groups.

title: SOCIAL WORK METHODS
description: An overview of the techniques that study or approach the understanding of individuals and their problems. The course will also examine techniques or approaches to social problem intervention at the societal level.

title: SOCIOLOGICAL INTERNSHIP
description: Offers the student practical experience with the Center for Learning and Research or with other agencies delivering social services. Ten hours per week of directed reading, agency work, and seminar.

title: SOCIAL STRATIFICATION
description: A study of the distribution of power, prestige, and position within society and its influence on behavior with emphasis on social stratification in the United States.

title: SOCIAL ORGANIZATION
description: Study of structure and function of large-scale organizations. Comparisons of various types of organizations.
prerequisites: SOCL 105 or SOCL 107 , or consent of the Department

title: POPULATION
description: Analysis of worldwide human population change that stresses ecological influences on social structure, plus an introduction to population methods and microcomputer simulation of social processes.
prerequisites: SOCL 105 or SOCL 107 , or consent of the Department

title: CRIMINOLOGY
description: Analysis of crime and its causes; theories of criminal and delinquent behavior.
prerequisites: SOCL 105 or SOCL 107 , or consent of the Department

title: JUVENILE DELINQUINCY
description: Study of juvenile delinquency, its causes, treatment, and prevention.
cross_listed: Also listed as CJ 322 and SW 322 .

title: RESEARCH METHODS IN THE SOCIAL SCIENCES
description: This course presents an overview of the basic methods and techniques used in social sciences research. It includes descriptive and inferential statistics, hypothesis testing, population and same identification, data collection, statistical analysis, and research report presentation with special emphasis on the areas of criminal justice, political science, and sociology.
cross_listed: Also listed as CJ 330 , NPA 330 and POLI 330 .

title: OVERVIEW OF AMERICAN SOCIAL WELFARE
description: The interrelatedness of social welfare delivery systems at the local, state, and national level.

title: RELIGIOUS STUDIES SEMINAR
description: The course may be repeated for a maximum of six term hours. Selected topics of significance in religious thought, literature, discipline, and history vary from term to term. Three hours of seminar.

title: RELIGIOUS STUDIES SEMINAR
description: Students will be expected to apply broad literary, theoretical, and cultural analyses to selected topics of significance in religious thought, literature, discipline, and history. These topics will vary from term to term. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: PATHWAYS TO SCIENCE AND HEALTH CAREERS
description: SC 100 features guest speakers from a wide variety of Science and Health-based professions who introduce students to a wide range of Science and Health careers. Speakers may include professionals from any branch of the Health Sciences, Biology, Chemistry, Physics, and Psychology. One hour lecture. May retaken as an elective or audited.

title: INTEGRATED SCIENCE I
description: An integrated approach to science with content centered on important concepts and relevant information. The process of science, organization of matter, energy and resources, and food preservation will be discussed. The course will utilize a combination of inquiry laboratories, small group discussions, and short lectures integrating all areas of science. This course is intended primarily for elementary education majors. A combination of six hours of lecture/laboratory per week.

title: INTEGRATED SCIENCE II
description: An integrated approach to science with content centered on important concepts and relevant information. Radioactivity, alternative energy sources and energy efficiency will be discussed. The course will utilize a combination of inquiry laboratories, small group discussions, and short lectures integrating all areas of science. This course is intended primarily for elementary education majors. A combination of six hours of lecture/laboratory per week.
prerequisites: SC 101 or consent of the College

title: INTEGRATED SCIENCE III
description: An integrated approach to science with content centered on important concepts and relevant information. Ecosystem structure and function, organization of life, nutrification, balance and imbalance in ecosystems, and competition within ecosystems will be discussed. The course will utilize a combination of inquiry laboratories, small group discussions, and short lectures integrating all areas of science. This course is intended primarily for elementary education majors. A combination of six hours of lecture/laboratory per week.
prerequisites: SC 102 or consent of the College

title: EARTH/SPACE SCIENCE FOR MIDDLE SCHOOL TEACHERS
description: A course intended primarily for current or future middle school science teachers. Covers forces that act on the structure and evolution of the earth and the universe, including landforms and topography, history, astronomy and space exploration. Four hours of lecture/lab.

title: HISTORY OF SCIENCE
description: This course covers the growth of science from Ancient Greece to the 21st century and its impact on the development of western civilization. Emphasis is placed on the biological, chemical, and physical sciences. Three hours of lecture.

title: CONTEMPORARY ISSUES IN SCIENCE
description: An investigation of contemporary issues that affect our society. An open forum for investigating the implications of decisions influenced by scientific realities and possibilities. One hour of seminar.

title: HISTORY OF SCIENCE
description: This course covers the growth of science from Ancient Greece to the 21st century and its impact on the development of western civilization. Emphasis is placed on the biological, chemical, and physical sciences. Three hours of lecture.

title: HISPANIC-AMERICAN CULTURE AND CIVILIZATION
description: A survey of the expression of the Hispanic-American personality through music, painting, and the plastic arts, and a study of the cultural factors that form the background for knowledgeable communication in Spanish.
prerequisites: SPAN 201 or consent of the Department

title: SURVEY OF SPANISH LITERATURE I
description: A study of the development of Spanish literature from its beginning through the eighteenth century.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish at the 300-level, or consent of the Department

title: SURVEY OF SPANISH LITERATURE II
description: Readings from main authors and study of main literary movements in Spanish literature from the beginning of the nineteenth century to the present.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish at the 300-level, or consent of the Department

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN SPANISH
description: Courses may include selected topics from Spanish, and Spanish literature, linguistics, culture, and the arts, or for specific professional fields. Topics will vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six hours.
prerequisites: SPAN 201 or consent of the Department

title: STUDY ABROAD
description: For students enrolled in an approved academic program in a Spanish-speaking country. Upon return, LSUS equivalency may be assigned for part or all of this work. Variable credit to a maximum of 15 term hours. Grading is on a pass/no credit basis. In some cases, tuition will need to be paid at a foreign university.
cross_listed: This course is the same as SPAN 292 except that LSUS credit is restricted to course work considered advanced.

title: CERVANTES AND THE GOLDEN AGE
description: A study of prose fiction, drama, and poetry of sixteenth and seventeenth century Spain. Special emphasis on Cervantes.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish literature at the 300-level, or consent of the Department

title: SPANISH CINEMA
description: The development of the cinema in Spain from the post-Civil War era to the present, with a close analysis of films by directors such as Luis Buñuel, Carlos Saura, Victor Érice and Pedro Almodóvar. Films are in Spanish with English subtitles, and lectures and class discussions are conducted in English.

title: LATIN AMERICAN CINEMA
description: The development of the cinema in Latin America from its origins to the present. Films are approached in a historical, sociopolitical, literary, and aesthetic context. Films are in Spanish with English subtitles, and lectures and class discussions are conducted in English.

title: SPANISH LITERATURE OF THE TWENTIETH CENTURY
description: A study of poetry, drama, and prose fiction in Spain from the Generation of 1898 to present.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish literature at the 300-level, or consent of the Department

title: SPANISH-AMERICAN LITERATURE I
description: A study of the development of Spanish- American literature from the colonial period through Modernism.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish literature at the 300-level, or consent of the Department

title: ELEMENTARY SPANISH I
description: A beginning course for students who have no preparation in Spanish. An introduction to the fundamentals of Spanish grammar, vocabulary, and syntax, with emphasis on the four skills of reading, writing, speaking, and listening.

title: ELEMENTARY SPANISH II
description: A continuation of SPAN 101 with continued emphasis on the aural-oral aspects of the language. Continuation of elementary reading selections.

title: INTERMEDIATE SPANISH
description: Continued practice in reading, writing, and speaking, along with vocabulary building and review of the basic principles of grammar.

title: READINGS IN SPANISH LITERATURE
description: Readings in classical and contemporary Spanish prose. Special emphasis on comprehension as well as oral and written expression of the language.
prerequisites: SPAN 201 or consent of the Department

title: STUDY ABROAD
description: For students enrolled in an approved academic program in a Spanish-speaking country. Upon return, LSUS equivalency may be assigned for part or all of this work. Variable credit to a maximum of 15 term hours. Grading is pass/no credit basis. In some cases, tuition will need to be paid at a foreign university.

title: SPANISH PHONETICS AND CONVERSATION
description: An analysis of the Spanish sound system and conversational practice. Emphasis on the special pronunciation difficulties experienced by English-speaking students of Spanish.
prerequisites: SPAN 201 or consent of the Department

title: BUSINESS SPANISH
description: Intensive oral and written practice in the language of business of Spain and Latin America. Topics include marketing, sales, accounting, finance, economics, letter writing, legal matters, business etiquette, and international business relations.
prerequisites: SPAN 201 or consent of the Department

title: ADVANCED SPANISH GRAMMAR AND COMPOSITION
description: Intensive study of Spanish syntax with extensive practice in oral and written communication and translation. Emphasis will be given to the special grammatical difficulties experienced by English-speaking students of Spanish.
prerequisites: SPAN 201 or consent of the Department

title: MEDICAL SPANISH
description: Intensive oral and written practice in Spanish as it is used in the health care professions. Emphasis on real- world situations and role-playing exercises leading to the linguistic precision necessary for these professions.

title: SPANISH CULTURE AND CIVILIZATON
description: A survey of the expression of the Spanish personality through music, painting, and the plastic arts, and a study of the cultural factors that form the background for knowledgeable communication in Spanish.
prerequisites: SPAN 201 or consent of the department

title: SPANISH-AMERICAN LITERATURE II
description: Readings from main authors and study of main literary movements in Spanish-American literature since Modernism. Three hours of lecture.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish literature at the 300-level, or consent of the Department

title: SEMINAR
description: Selected topics from Latin American and Spanish literature and linguistics will vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish at the 300-level, or consent of the Department

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES IN SPANISH
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the direction of a member of the Spanish faculty. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.
prerequisites: Three hours of Spanish literature at the 300-level, and consent of the Department

title: CERVANTES AND THE GOLDEN AGE
description: A study of prose, fiction, drama, and poetry of sixteenth and seventeenth century Spain. Special emphasis on Cervantes and his influence on literature and literary studies in Europe and America. The course includes an examination of various critical and theoretical approaches to the texts. Three hours of lecture.

title: SPANISH CINEMA
description: The development of the cinema in Spain from the post-Civil War era to the present, with a close analysis of films by directors such as Luis Buñuel, Carlos Saura, Victor Érice and Pedro Almodóvar. The course examines cinematographic schools, techniques, and innovations, as well as critical and theoretical approaches to the films. Lectures are conducted in English, and films are in Spanish with English subtitles. Written and oral assignments may be completed in English or Spanish. Three hours of lecture.

title: LATIN AMERICAN CINEMA
description: The development of the cinema in Latin America from its origins to the present. Films are approached in a historical, sociopolitical, literary, and aesthetic context with particular attention to different theoretical and critical approaches to the cinematographic texts. Lectures are conducted in English, and films are in Spanish with English subtitles. Written and oral assignments may be completed in English or Spanish. Three hours of lecture.

title: SPANISH LITERATURE OF THE TWENTIETH CENTURY
description: A study of poetry, drama, and prose fiction in Spain from the Generation of 1898 to present. The course includes literary analysis within a socio-historical context, along with an examination of a variety of critical and theoretical approaches to the texts. Three hours of lecture.

title: SPANISH-AMERICAN LITERATURE I
description: A study of the development of Spanish-American literature from the colonial period through Modernism. The course includes literary analysis within a socio-historical context, along with an examination of a variety of critical and theoretical approaches to the texts. Three hours of lecture.

title: SPANISH-AMERICAN LITERATURE II
description: Readings from main authors and study of main literary movements in Spanish-American literature since Modernism. Students will analyze the texts within a socio-historical context, using a variety of critical and theoretical approaches. Three hours of lecture.

title: SEMINAR
description: Selected topics from Latin American and Spanish literature and linguistics will vary from term to term and will include an in-depth study of related literary history and/or theory. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: ADVANCED ACTING
description: An advanced acting class that concentrates on text analysis, the application of theory to practice, cultural diversity, and superior physical and vocal production. Performance required.
prerequisites: THEA 361 or equivalent, or consent of the Department

title: THEATRE AND FILM
description: The relationship between theatre and film, including theatre as the original foundation for film and multimedia in theatre.

title: THE ART OF DIRECTING
description: This course explores the auteur theory, the concept that the key artist in film is the director. Students will learn how to compare and contrast the narrative, expressive and representative methods of key directors.

title: COLLABORATION IN THEATRE
description: Students are introduced to theories and practices involved in collaboration in theatrical rehearsal and performance. Students learn basic methods for successful collaboration between the director, actors, designers, the stage manager, technicians and others such as playwrights, choreographers, composers, etc. Students receive practical experience through the preparation of scripted and improvised scenes and a group project.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture and three hours of lab'}

title: LEARNING STRATEGIES FOR COLLEGE SUCCESS
description: A course designed to help the student acquire and sharpen the skills necessary for success in college. Includes learning and memory, motivation, test anxiety, reading for comprehension, time budgeting, and other skills.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'One hour of lecture and one hour of lab'}

title: UNIVERSITY WORK SERVICE
description: The course consists of a minimum of 120 hours of employment with area businesses and organizations. An orientation session, self-paced modules, and a written final report are required. Three hours of credit on a pass/no credit basis.
prerequisites: Completion of 24 credit hours with a 2.2 cumulative GPA

title: STUDY ABROAD SEMINAR
description: A study abroad seminar surveying selected international business activities and environments. The course uses a combination of theoretical and applied formats to expose students to selected foreign cultural, historical, and social phenomena and their role in the global context. May be repeated for an additional three credit hours if the destination is different.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture, applied case studies, and field trips'}

title: WOMEN LEADERS
description: This course examines women leaders from a historical and analytical point of view, with attention to theories of leadership and their application to specific individuals.

title: IMAGES OF WOMEN
description: An exploration of the ways in which women have been depicted in literature and art as a response to descriptions of gender and gender roles in religion, philosophy, and psychological theory from the classical period through modern times.

title: WOMEN IN THE AMERICAS
description: A study of women from different historical periods, racial and ethnic backgrounds, and areas of achievement, who have made significant contributions to the culture(s) of the Americas.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES IN SPANISH
description: Readings, conferences, reports, and a learning project emphasizing critical thinking under the direction of a member of the Spanish faculty. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN SPANISH LITERATURE IN TRANSLATION
description: Special topics selected from areas such as major authors, periods, genres, influences, and critical theories. Topics vary from term to term. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN EUROPEAN LITERATURE IN TRANSLATION
description: Course is designed to acquaint students with masterpieces of Spanish and other non-English European literature(s). In-depth study of special topics (major authors, periods, genres, problems or domestic questions) varies from term to term. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 term hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: APPLIED BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS IN EDUCATION
description: This course explores tools and processes for intervention, evaluation, and data collection in leading behavior change work in schools and districts. Through extension of basic behavioral principles and exploration of research in applied behavior analysis, candidates will address issues including behavioral assessment, goal development, selection of behavior change procedures, ethical and legal responsibilities, leading behavior change work, and program evaluation. Three hours of lecture.

title: LEGAL AND ETHICAL ISSUES IN SPECIAL EDUCATION
description: This course prepares candidates for leadership in special education through exploration of federal and state policy, working with federal programs, and understanding finance in special education. The student will examine federal and state legislation, case law, and regulations regarding compliance and services to children with disabilities.

title: ADMINISTRATION AND SUPERVISION OF SPECIAL EDUCATION
description: This course prepares candidates for leadership in special education through exploration of administrative and supervision issues working with special populations. The candidate will examine topics in leadership and management, organizational planning and change, and strategic thinking regarding services to children with disabilities.

title: INTRODUCTION TO SOCIAL WORK
description: A general introduction to the field and profession of social work. Historical survey, description of social welfare programs in contemporary American society, and the roles of the social worker in meeting social needs. Three hours of lecture.

title: SOCIAL WORK METHODS
description: An overview of the techniques that study or approach the understanding of individuals and their problems. The course will also examine techniques or approaches to social problem intervention at the societal level. Three hours of lecture.

title: JUVENILE DELINQUENCY
description: Study of delinquency, its causes, treatment, and prevention. Three hours of lecture.
cross_listed: Also listed as SOCL 322 and CJ 322 .

title: OVERVIEW OF AMERICAN SOCIAL WELFARE
description: The interrelatedness of social welfare delivery systems at the local, state, and national level. Three hours of lecture.

title: VOICE FOR PERFORMANCE
description: Assists students in achieving professional skills in vocal quality, diction, intonation, and pronunciation.
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Three hours of lecture.', 'louisiana_common_course': 'CTHE 2203/Voice for the Stage'}

title: INTRODUCTION TO THEATRE
description: An introduction to the theatre as an art form and to dramatic structure, forms, and style. The course emphasizes the collaborative nature of this art form.

title: HISTORY OF THE THEATRE THROUGH THE RENAISSANCE
description: The historical development of the theatre from primitive tribes through the Greeks and the Renaissance.

title: HISTORY OF THE THEATRE SINCE THE RENAISSANCE
description: The historical development of the theatre from the 16th century to the present including the Avant-garde and Absurdist movements.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ACTING
description: A beginning acting class that concentrates on body awareness, voice production, and acting techniques.

title: THEATRE LABORATORY
description: Participation in LSUS-sponsored or approved off-campus theatrical productions as a performer or technical staff member. Student will keep record of and analyze involvement in theatrical experiences. May be repeated for a maximum of three hours.

title: THEATRE PRODUCTION
description: Teaches students about constructing theatre sets, implementing lighting designs, creating costumes, applying makeup, and handling properties. Students will be actively involved as crew members for LSUS productions or other approved productions.
prerequisites: THEA 160 or equivalent, or Instructor permission
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.'}

title: PERSONAL PERFORMANCE
description: An advanced class in expressive communication designed to assist students in achieving professional skills in the analysis and personal performance of drama, poetry, fiction, prose, and performance art.
prerequisites: THEA 125 or COMM 135 or equivalent, or consent of the Department

title: INTERMEDIATE ACTING
description: An intermediate acting class that concentrates on text analysis, psychological and emotional investigation, and effective physical and vocal production. Performance required.
prerequisites: THEA 261 or equivalent, or consent of the Department

title: DIRECTING FOR THE STAGE
description: This course focuses on the theory and practice of selecting, designing, rehearsing, and presenting a play for public exhibition. Students will learn to analyze scripts, design appropriate stage environments, and cast and rehearse actors.
prerequisites: THEA 160 or consent of the Department
others: {'lecture_hours': 'Two hours of lecture and two hours of lab.'}

title: WOMEN AND SPORT
description: A broad-based survey of topics relevant to girls’ and women’s participation in sport. Topics include gender issues; major historical female figures in women’s sport; government and policy, including the struggle for Title IX; media representation of women’s sports; Olympics and women’s sports; physiological and psychological perspectives; and future of women’s sports.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS
description: Special topics selected from areas such as major authors, genres, periods, influences, or critical theories. Topics vary from term to term.

title: WOMEN LEADERS
description: This course examines women leaders from a historical and analytical point of view, with attention to theories of leadership and their application to specific individuals. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations.

title: IMAGES OF WOMEN
description: An exploration of the ways in which women have been depicted in literature and art as a response to descriptions of gender and gender roles in religion, philosophy, and psychological theory from the classical period through modern times. Students will analyze the texts within a socio-cultural context, using a variety of critical and theoretical approaches.

title: WOMEN IN THE AMERICAS
description: A study of women from different historical periods, racial and ethnic backgrounds, and areas of achievement, who have made significant contributions to the culture(s) of the Americas. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations.

title: WOMEN AND SPORT
description: A broad-based survey of topics relevant to girls’ and women’s participation in sport. Topics include gender issues; major historical female figures in women’s sports; government and policy, including the struggle for Title IX; media representation of women’s sports; Olympics and women’s sports; physiological and psychological perspectives; and the future of women’s sports. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS
description: Special topics concerning women, which may be selected from areas such as major authors, genres, periods, influences, or critical theories. Topics vary from term to term. Assesses advanced critical thinking skills through scholarly reading and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN WOMEN’S STUDIES
description: Topics vary from semester to semester. This course explores a major author, movement, period, theory, genre or other thematic issue concerning women. May be repeated for up to 6 hours of credit.

title: FIELD RESEARCH
description: The student will be placed in a social agency and given individual supervision. Also, the student will meet in a group once each week throughout the term. Three hours of research.

title: SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS
description: The analysis and application of major theories of social institutions such as education, family, politics and religion. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: SOCIAL CHANGE AND THE FUTURE
description: The analysis and application of major theories of social change processes to speculate about the nature of future post-industrial or information/services societies. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: SOCIOLOGICAL THEORY
description: A study of the major paradigms of sociological theory through a historical perspective and an examination of selected proponents. Students will analyze and compare their underlying assumptions, explanatory variables, and conceptual framework of their theories. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: SOCIOLOGY OF HEALTH
description: As historical analysis of the health status of various population subgroups within the United States. Students will analyze various theories of health determinants, management of health risks, and health behaviors in diverse populations. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. Three hours of lecture.

title: SEMINAR
description: Selected topics from current issues and problems in sociology, will vary from term to term. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis, as well as through oral presentations. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the guidance of a member of the Sociology faculty. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis. Three hours of research.

title: FIELD RESEARCH
description: The student will be placed in a social agency and given individual supervision; also, students will research the history and development of the agency. Assesses critical thinking skills through scholarly readings and written analysis. Three hours of research.

title: SOCIOLOGY OF EDUCATION
description: Examination of the social structure and culture of schools, and of the interrelationships between educational institutions and society. Three hours of lecture.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN SOCIOLOGY
description: Special topics are selected from areas such as theory, stratification, and demography. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six hours. Three hours of seminar.

title: PRACTICUM IN NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Explores strategic planning, marketing, legal issues, program assessment, fundraising, development, financial planning, risk management, social media, human resources management, and organizational communication within nonprofit organizations and the particular challenges involved differing from for-profit organizations.
cross_listed: Also listed as NPA 392 and POLI 392 .

title: SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS
description: Analysis of major social institutions such as education, family, politics, and religion.
prerequisites: SOCL 105 or SOCL 107 , or consent of the Department

title: SOCIAL CHANGE AND THE FUTURE
description: Use of a knowledge of social change processes to speculate about the nature of future post-industrial or information/services societies.
prerequisites: SOCL 105 or SOCL 107 , or consent of the Department

title: SOCIOLOGICAL THEORY
description: A study of the major paradigms of sociological theory through a historical perspective and an examination of selected proponents. These proponents will be discussed in terms of their underlying assumptions, explanatory variables, and conceptual framework of their theories.

title: SOCIOLOGY OF HEALTH
description: An assessment of the health status of various population subgroups within the United States with an emphasis on the determinants of health, management of health risks, and health behaviors in diverse populations.

title: SEMINAR
description: Selected topics from current issues and problems in sociology will vary from term to term. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: SENIOR SEMINAR IN SOCIOLOGY
description: The capstone course of the Sociology program. Faculty will guide students on a project challenging their application of sociological insight, research methodology, and data analysis.

title: SEMINAR IN NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Designed as a preparatory for student internships in not-for-profit organizations by having them solve problems in areas such as finances, personnel, marketing, and risk management in a simulated organization. There will be individual/group projects.
cross_listed: Also listed as NPA 492 and POLI 492 .

title: AMERICAN HUMANICS MANAGEMENT TRAINING INSTITUTE
description: Participation in the annual meeting of the American Humanics Management Training Institute. Funds for attendance are to be raised by the student.
cross_listed: Also listed as MADM 493 and POLI 493 .

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the guidance of a member of the sociology faculty. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of six term hours.

title: THEATRE & FILM
description: This course examines the relationship between theatre and film, including theatre as the original foundation for film and multimedia in theatre.

title: ART OF DIRECTING
description: This course explores the auteur theory, the concept that the key artist in film is the director. Students will learn how to compare and contrast the narrative, expressive, and representative methods of key directors.

title: POLITICS IN THE CINEMA
description: Investigates how political communication occurs through film, the messages sent through film, and the influence of film in shaping or altering political attitudes.

title: STUDY IN FILM
description: A variable topics course for advanced students interested in studying a topic more deeply than regular offerings permit. May focus on a major director, a film era, a genre, film criticism, film theory, or pedagogy. May be repeated for credit.

title: FOREIGN LANGUAGE I
description: A beginning course for students who have no preparation in the language. An introduction to the fundamentals of grammar, vocabulary, and syntax, with an emphasis on the four skills of reading, writing, speaking, and listening. May be repeated for credit in a different language for a maximum of six hours.

title: FOREIGN LANGUAGE II
description: A continuation of FL 101 with continued emphasis on the auraloral aspects of the language. Continuation of elementary reading selections and writing assignments.
prerequisites: FL 101 or consent of the Department

title: FOREIGN LANGUAGE III
description: Continued reading and oral work, vocabulary building, further development of writing skills, and review of the basic principles of grammar.
prerequisites: FL 102 or consent of the Department

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the direction of a member of the foreign language faculty. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours.

title: INDEPENDENT STUDIES
description: Readings, conferences, and reports under the direction of a member of the foreign language faculty. May be repeated for a maximum of six term hours.

title: ELEMENTARY FRENCH I
description: A beginning course for students who have no preparation in French. An introduction to the fundamentals of French grammar, vocabulary, and syntax, with emphasis on the four skills of reading, writing, speaking, and listening.
